case_name: Costa v ENEL
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: European Community law takes precedence over the national law of Member States, including subsequent national legislation. Member States have permanently limited their sovereign rights by acceding to the Community, and cannot invoke their own national provisions against the application of Community law. Supremacy of EU law is inherent in the nature of the Treaty.
key_quote: The law stemming from the Treaty... could not, because of its special and original nature, be overridden by domestic legal provisions, however framed, without being deprived of its character as Community law.
full_summary: Mr Costa refused to pay his electricity bill after Italy nationalised its electricity company (ENEL), arguing the nationalisation law conflicted with the EEC Treaty. The Italian Government argued the EEC Treaty could not override a later Italian statute. The Court of Justice established the fundamental doctrine of the supremacy of Community (now EU) law over the national law of Member States. Member States have transferred legislative powers to the Community and cannot therefore invoke against Community law their own subsequently enacted legislation. A uniform application of Community law would be impossible if each Member State could apply only those rules of Community law that do not conflict with national law. Costa v ENEL is the single most important EU Law case for FE-1, establishing the constitutional foundation of the EU legal order. It must be read with Van Gend en Loos (direct effect) to understand the twin pillars of EU constitutionalism.

case_name: Amministrazione delle Finanze dello Stato v Simmenthal SpA
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Every national court has a duty to give full effect to EU law and must of its own motion disapply any conflicting national provision, without waiting for that provision to be set aside by the legislature or by a higher national court. The national court must not seek a prior ruling from its own constitutional court before disapplying the national law.
key_quote: Every national court must, in a case within its jurisdiction, apply Community law in its entirety and protect rights which the latter confers on individuals and must accordingly set aside any provision of national law which may conflict with it.
full_summary: Italian health inspection fees were charged on imported beef contrary to Community law. When the case came before an Italian court, the Italian Government argued that only the Italian Constitutional Court could declare an Italian law incompatible with Community law. The ECJ held that every national court must disapply conflicting national law directly, immediately, and without waiting for its repeal or for a ruling from the Constitutional Court. The supremacy of EU law requires that its legal effects be uniform across all Member States and in all courts. A national court that must await a higher ruling before disapplying national law would be depriving EU law of its effectiveness. Simmenthal operationalises the supremacy principle from Costa v ENEL by specifying the practical obligation on national judges. It is essential FE-1 authority on the duty of national courts under the supremacy doctrine.

case_name: Internationale Handelsgesellschaft mbH v Einfuhr- und Vorratsstelle für Getreide
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The validity of a Community measure or its effect within a Member State cannot be affected by allegations that it runs counter to the fundamental rights enshrined in that State's constitution. The supremacy of Community law extends even over constitutional provisions of Member States, though the Court recognised that fundamental rights form general principles of Community law.
key_quote: The validity of Community measures can only be judged in the light of Community law itself. The introduction of special criteria for validity emanating from the legislation or constitutional law of a particular Member State would... impair the unity and efficacy of Community law.
full_summary: A German company challenged a Community regulation requiring a deposit as a condition for export licences, arguing it violated fundamental rights guaranteed by the German Basic Law. The ECJ held that the validity of Community acts could not be assessed by reference to the constitutional law of any Member State, including its fundamental rights provisions — otherwise the uniformity of Community law would be destroyed. However, the Court acknowledged for the first time that fundamental rights form part of the general principles of Community law which the Court must protect, drawing inspiration from the constitutional traditions of Member States. This creates a two-way dynamic: Community law trumps national constitutions, but Community law itself is subject to fundamental rights as general principles. This is a seminal FE-1 case on supremacy, the Solange controversy, and the development of EU fundamental rights.

case_name: R v Secretary of State for Transport, ex parte Factortame Ltd (Factortame I)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National courts must provide effective interim relief to protect rights derived from EU law, even where no such remedy exists in national law. A national rule which prevents a national court from granting interim relief must be set aside if it is the only obstacle to effective protection of EU rights.
key_quote: Community law must be interpreted as meaning that a national court... must set aside a rule of national law which prevents it from granting interim relief in order to ensure the full effectiveness of the judgment to be given.
full_summary: Spanish fishing companies challenged the Merchant Shipping Act 1988 (UK) which prevented them from fishing under UK quotas, in alleged breach of EU law. They sought interim relief (suspension of the Act) while the main case was pending. The House of Lords referred to the ECJ, as English courts had no power to suspend an Act of Parliament. The ECJ held that EU law requires national courts to set aside national procedural rules (including the constitutional principle of parliamentary sovereignty in the UK) if those rules prevent effective judicial protection of EU rights. This was the first time a UK court suspended an Act of Parliament. Factortame I is the most dramatic demonstration of the supremacy of EU law over national constitutional law and is a critical FE-1 case on the relationship between EU law and the UK (and Irish) legal orders.

case_name: NV Algemene Transport- en Expeditie Onderneming van Gend en Loos v Nederlandse Administratie der Belastingen
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Provisions of Community (EU) law that are clear, precise, and unconditional confer rights on individuals which national courts must protect, independently of any implementing measure by Member States. This is the doctrine of direct effect. Treaty articles may be directly effective both vertically (against the State) and, in some cases, horizontally (against private parties).
key_quote: The Community constitutes a new legal order of international law for the benefit of which the states have limited their sovereign rights... and the subjects of which comprise not only Member States but also their nationals.
full_summary: A Dutch transport company challenged a Dutch customs duty on a product imported from West Germany, arguing it contravened Article 12 of the EEC Treaty (now Article 30 TFEU) which prohibited increases in customs duties. The ECJ held that Article 12 was directly effective: it was clear, precise, and unconditional. The EEC Treaty was more than an ordinary international agreement — it created a new legal order whose subjects included individuals as well as Member States. Individuals could therefore rely on directly effective Treaty provisions before national courts. Van Gend en Loos (with Costa v ENEL) forms the twin constitutional foundation of EU law. It is the single most important case for understanding EU law's unique nature, and is examined in virtually every EU Law FE-1 paper. The three criteria for direct effect (clear, precise, unconditional) are the core learning point.

case_name: Defrenne v Sabena (No 2)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Where a Treaty provision is sufficiently clear and precise, it may be directly effective both vertically (against the State and its emanations) and horizontally (against private individuals and companies). The principle of equal pay for men and women in Article 119 EEC (now Article 157 TFEU) was held to have horizontal direct effect.
key_quote: The principle of equal pay forms part of the foundations of the Community. Since this provision is mandatory in nature, the prohibition on discrimination between men and women applies not only to the action of public authorities but also to all agreements which are intended to regulate paid labour collectively.
full_summary: Gabrielle Defrenne (an air hostess employed by Sabena, a private Belgian airline) was paid less than male colleagues doing the same work. She relied on Article 119 EEC (equal pay) against her private employer. The ECJ held Article 119 had horizontal direct effect: its prohibition on unequal pay applied not only against the State but also against private employers and collective labour agreements. This was a major extension of Van Gend en Loos: Treaty provisions with direct effect can be relied upon against private parties. However, the Court limited the temporal effect of its ruling (it would not be retroactively applied to past pay claims). Defrenne (No 2) is the leading authority on horizontal direct effect of Treaty provisions. It is contrasted with the no-horizontal-direct-effect rule for Directives (Marshall v Southampton).

case_name: Van Duyn v Home Office
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Directives may be directly effective once the implementation deadline has passed, provided the provision is sufficiently clear, precise, and unconditional. Direct effect of Directives allows individuals to rely on unimplemented or incorrectly implemented Directive provisions against the State (vertical direct effect).
key_quote: It would be incompatible with the binding effect attributed to a Directive by Article 189 to exclude in principle the possibility that the obligation it imposes may be invoked by those concerned.
full_summary: Ms Van Duyn, a Dutch national and member of the Church of Scientology, was refused leave to enter the UK to take up employment at the Church's headquarters. The UK Government relied on a Directive (64/221/EEC) concerning restrictions on movement of workers on grounds of public policy, health, or security. The ECJ held for the first time that Directives (as well as Treaty provisions) could be directly effective. Article 189 EEC made Directives binding on Member States as to their result, and it would undermine the effectiveness of Directives if individuals could not rely on them before national courts. The provision in question was sufficiently clear and precise. Van Duyn establishes the principle of direct effect of Directives, the third key tool (with Treaty direct effect and regulations) available to individuals in national courts.

case_name: Pubblico Ministero v Tullio Ratti
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Member State that has failed to implement a Directive by the deadline may not rely on its own failure (defaulting on its obligation) against an individual who has complied with the Directive's requirements. The estoppel principle: the State is estopped from relying on its own unlawful failure to implement.
key_quote: A Member State which has not adopted the implementing measures required by the Directive in the prescribed period may not rely, as against individuals, on its own failure to perform the obligations which the Directive entails.
full_summary: Ratti was an Italian manufacturer of solvents who labelled his products in accordance with two EEC Directives. Italian law required different labelling. He was prosecuted under Italian law. For one Directive (whose implementation deadline had passed), Italy could not rely on its own non-implementation to prosecute Ratti. For another Directive (whose deadline had not yet passed), Italy could still enforce its own law. The ECJ developed the 'estoppel' justification for Directive direct effect: a Member State cannot invoke its own breach of EU law (failure to implement) against an individual who has complied with the Directive. This reasoning explains why Directive direct effect is vertical only — the estoppel argument works only against the State. It cannot be used against a private party who is not responsible for the non-implementation.

case_name: Marshall v Southampton and South-West Hampshire Area Health Authority (Teaching)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Directive cannot of itself impose obligations on a private individual; it can only be relied upon against the State and its emanations (vertical direct effect only). There is no horizontal direct effect of Directives. A State and its emanations include public authorities such as health authorities.
key_quote: A Directive may not of itself impose obligations on an individual and, accordingly, a provision of a Directive may not be relied upon as such against such a person.
full_summary: Miss Marshall, employed by Southampton Area Health Authority (a public body), was dismissed at age 62 under a policy that women must retire at 60 while men could work until 65. She relied on the Equal Treatment Directive (76/207/EEC). The ECJ held the Directive was directly effective against the health authority as an emanation of the State. However, the Court crucially stated that a Directive cannot impose obligations on private individuals — it can only be used against the State (vertical direct effect). The limitation to vertical direct effect means individuals cannot rely on Directives against private employers or individuals. This limitation drives the development of indirect effect (Marleasing) and State liability (Francovich) as substitutes for horizontal direct effect. Marshall is the definitive authority on the no-horizontal-direct-effect-of-directives rule.

case_name: Foster v British Gas plc
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: An emanation of the State (against which a Directive may be directly enforced) includes a body which provides a public service under State control, has special powers beyond those normally applicable between individuals, and whose service is controlled by the State. The Foster criteria determine whether a body is a State emanation.
key_quote: A body, whatever its legal form, which has been made responsible pursuant to a measure adopted by the State for providing a public service under the control of the State and has for that purpose special powers beyond those which result from the normal rules applicable in relations between individuals is included in any event among the bodies against which the provisions of a Directive capable of having direct effect may be relied upon.
full_summary: Foster and colleagues were employed by British Gas (then a nationalised industry) and forced to retire at 60 while their male counterparts could work until 65, in breach of the Equal Treatment Directive. British Gas was subsequently privatised. The ECJ confirmed British Gas in its pre-privatisation state was an emanation of the State: it had a statutory duty to provide a public service, was under State control, and had special statutory powers (e.g., to search for gas). The three-part Foster test allows individuals to invoke Directives against bodies that are not formally the State but exercise State functions. This is the leading case on the 'emanation of the State' concept, critical for FE-1 analysis of when Directives can be invoked against semi-private bodies.

case_name: Faccini Dori v Recreb Srl
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Directives cannot have horizontal direct effect — they cannot be relied upon as such against a private party in national proceedings. The Court expressly declined to extend Directive direct effect horizontally, even when the Directive in question protected consumers from private traders. The remedy for the gap left by no horizontal direct effect is indirect effect and State liability.
key_quote: Extending the case law on direct effect to the sphere of relations between individuals would be to recognise a power in the Community to enact obligations for individuals with immediate effect, whereas it has competence to do so only where it is empowered to adopt Regulations.
full_summary: Ms Faccini Dori entered into a contract for an English language correspondence course at Milan railway station. She sought to cancel within the 7-day cooling off period guaranteed by the Doorstep Selling Directive (85/577/EEC), but Italy had not implemented the Directive. The trader (Recreb) was a private company. The ECJ firmly refused to extend Directive direct effect horizontally. A Directive creates obligations for Member States only — not for individuals. To allow Directives to impose obligations on individuals would be to equate Directives with Regulations without the formal requirements (publication in OJ, etc.). The Court instead pointed to indirect effect (Marleasing) and State liability (Francovich) as remedies. Faccini Dori is the definitive restatement of the no-horizontal-direct-effect rule and is crucial FE-1 authority alongside Marshall v Southampton.

case_name: Wells v Secretary of State for Transport, Local Government and the Regions
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Even where a Directive would impose obligations on a private party as a consequence of the State's own obligations, the State cannot avoid those consequences by pleading that performance would adversely affect the private party. The principle that obligations cannot be imposed on private parties by Directives does not prevent a private party from suffering adverse repercussions from the correct application of EU law.
key_quote: Adverse repercussions on the rights of third parties, even if the repercussions are certain, do not justify preventing an individual from invoking the provisions of a Directive against the Member State concerned.
full_summary: Mrs Wells sought judicial review of the Government's failure to require an environmental impact assessment (EIA) for a quarry's resumption of operations, contrary to the EIA Directive. The quarry operators (private parties) might lose the right to continue operating if the Directive were applied. The ECJ held that Mrs Wells could rely on the Directive against the State. The fact that the quarry operators would suffer adverse effects from the State complying with its obligations under the Directive did not prevent her from invoking it. There is a distinction between 'horizontal direct effect' (imposing obligations on private parties) and 'inverse vertical' situations (where a private party suffers because of the State's compliance with a Directive). This case is significant FE-1 material on the outer boundaries of Directive direct effect.

case_name: Von Colson and Kamann v Land Nordrhein-Westfalen
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National courts are required to interpret their national law in the light of the wording and purpose of a Directive, so as to give effect to the Directive's objectives as far as possible. This duty of consistent interpretation (indirect effect) applies to all national courts when applying or construing national law, whether enacted before or after the Directive.
key_quote: It is for the national court to interpret and apply the legislation adopted for the implementation of the Directive in conformity with the requirements of Community law, in so far as it is given discretion to do so under national law.
full_summary: Von Colson and Kamann were rejected for prison service jobs in breach of the Equal Treatment Directive (76/207/EEC). German law offered only a minimal remedy (reimbursement of travel expenses). The ECJ held the Directive required effective remedies. The obligation to achieve the Directive's result fell on all national authorities including courts — courts must interpret national implementing measures in conformity with the Directive's objectives. This is the origin of the indirect effect (or consistent interpretation) doctrine. The duty is addressed to national courts and requires them to interpret national law 'as far as possible' in conformity with Directive requirements. Von Colson laid the foundation for the fuller doctrine developed in Marleasing.

case_name: Marleasing SA v La Comercial Internacional de Alimentación SA
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National courts must interpret their national law, whether enacted before or after a Directive, as far as possible in the light of the wording and purpose of the Directive in order to achieve the result pursued by the Directive. This obligation of consistent interpretation (indirect effect or Marleasing principle) applies even where the national law predates the Directive.
key_quote: In applying national law, whether the provisions in question were adopted before or after the Directive, the national court called upon to interpret it is required to do so, as far as possible, in the light of the wording and purpose of the Directive in order to achieve the result pursued by the latter.
full_summary: Marleasing sought to have La Comercial's articles of incorporation declared void under Spanish civil law on the ground that the company's formation was a sham. La Comercial argued the Company Law Directive (68/151/EEC) limited the grounds on which company incorporations could be declared void (sham was not a listed ground). Spain had not implemented the Directive. The ECJ extended Von Colson: national courts must interpret all national law 'as far as possible' in conformity with the Directive's requirements, regardless of whether the national law was enacted before or after the Directive, and regardless of whether the Directive has been implemented. The 'as far as possible' qualifier (contra legem limitation) prevents the duty from requiring an interpretation that is impossible given the national law's clear wording. Marleasing is the leading authority on indirect effect and is essential FE-1 learning alongside Marshall and Faccini Dori.

case_name: Océano Grupo Editorial SA v Murciano Quintero
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The duty of consistent interpretation requires national courts to interpret national law in the light of a Directive even in civil proceedings between private parties. Where national law can be interpreted in several ways, the national court must prefer the interpretation consistent with the Directive.
key_quote: The national court is bound to interpret its national law in the light of the Directive even in civil proceedings between private parties, where such an interpretation is possible.
full_summary: Océano Grupo Editorial brought debt claims against Spanish consumers in Spanish courts. The consumers claimed that jurisdiction clauses in their book purchase agreements were unfair under the Unfair Terms Directive (93/13/EEC). Spain had not fully implemented the Directive. The ECJ held that in civil proceedings between private parties (horizontal context), national courts must interpret their procedural and substantive rules 'as far as possible' in the light of the Directive. Even though the Directive could not be directly relied on horizontally (no horizontal direct effect), consistent interpretation filled the gap. The case is an important modern example of indirect effect in action in private consumer disputes.

case_name: Pupino
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The obligation of consistent interpretation (indirect effect) extends beyond Directives to Framework Decisions adopted under the third pillar of the old Treaty on European Union (justice and home affairs). National courts must interpret their national criminal procedural law in conformity with Framework Decisions as far as possible.
key_quote: The principle of interpretation in conformity with Community law must be applied in the area of police and judicial cooperation in criminal matters, with due regard for the general principles of law, including legal certainty and non-retroactivity.
full_summary: An Italian teacher (Pupino) was accused of inflicting harm on young pupils. A Framework Decision on victims' rights required Member States to allow special measures for vulnerable witnesses. Italian criminal procedure did not allow such measures in the relevant circumstances. The ECJ applied the indirect effect principle to Framework Decisions (which, unlike Directives, cannot have direct effect under the old EU Treaty but also bind Member States). The duty of consistent interpretation applies — national courts must interpret national criminal procedure law in the light of Framework Decisions as far as possible. The extension of indirect effect beyond the Community pillar into justice and home affairs cooperation is significant for the scope of the Marleasing principle.

case_name: Dominguez v Centre Informatique du Centre Ouest Atlantique
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The obligation of consistent interpretation has limits: a national court cannot interpret national law in a manner that is contra legem (against the law's clear meaning). Where consistent interpretation is impossible, the national court must set aside conflicting national law if the claim is against the State, or refer the individual to a claim in State liability if the defendant is private.
key_quote: The national court must do whatever lies within its jurisdiction to ensure the application of the Directive, disapplying national law if necessary, but it cannot apply a contra legem interpretation of national law to achieve that result.
full_summary: Dominguez sought paid annual leave under the Working Time Directive (2003/88/EC) but French law imposed conditions not present in the Directive. Her employer was a private body. The ECJ confirmed the limits of indirect effect: national courts cannot interpret national law contra legem (against its clear meaning). If consistent interpretation is impossible and the defendant is a State emanation, national law must be disapplied (direct effect). If the defendant is private and consistent interpretation is impossible, the individual is left with a Francovich State liability claim. The case is significant FE-1 authority mapping the interaction between direct effect, indirect effect, and State liability in a systematic fashion.

case_name: Francovich and Bonifaci v Italy
citation: Cases C-6/90 and C-9/90 [1991] ECR I-5357
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Member States are required to make good loss and damage caused to individuals by breaches of EU law attributable to the State. State liability arises where: (1) the EU law provision infringed was intended to confer rights on individuals; (2) the breach is sufficiently serious; and (3) there is a direct causal link between the breach and the damage. This establishes the doctrine of State liability (Francovich liability).
key_quote: The principle of State liability for loss and damage caused to individuals as a result of breaches of Community law for which the State can be held responsible is inherent in the system of the Treaty.
full_summary: Italy had failed to implement the Insolvency Directive (80/987/EEC) which required Member States to guarantee payment of employees' arrears of wages in the event of employer insolvency. Francovich and Bonifaci were owed wages by insolvent employers and could not get them. The ECJ held that Italy was required to pay compensation to Francovich and Bonifaci for its failure to implement the Directive. The principle of State liability is inherent in the EU legal order: effectiveness of EU law and the protection of rights created by EU law would be undermined if individuals could not obtain compensation for breaches caused by Member States. Francovich created the third remedy (alongside direct effect and indirect effect) available to individuals when EU law is breached. It is fundamental FE-1 material on remedies for breach of EU law.

case_name: Brasserie du Pêcheur SA v Germany; R v Secretary of State for Transport, ex parte Factortame Ltd (No 3)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The three conditions for State liability (established in Francovich) apply even where the breach involves a superior rule of EU law (such as a Treaty provision), not only failure to implement Directives. The key condition is that the breach must be 'sufficiently serious', meaning a manifest and grave disregard by the Member State of the limits of its discretion.
key_quote: The three conditions — the provision infringed was intended to confer rights on individuals, the breach was sufficiently serious, and there was a direct causal link — are necessary and sufficient to establish State liability.
full_summary: Two joined cases: Brasserie (a French brewery suing Germany for breach of the Purity Law restricting beer imports contrary to Article 30 EEC) and Factortame (Spanish fishermen suing the UK for breach of Treaty rights through the Merchant Shipping Act 1988). Both involved breaches of directly effective Treaty provisions (not just failure to implement Directives). The ECJ applied and extended Francovich: the three conditions for State liability apply regardless of which organ of the State caused the breach (legislature, executive, judiciary) and regardless of whether the breach was of a Directive or a Treaty provision. 'Sufficiently serious' requires a manifest and grave disregard of the Member State's discretion — factors include clarity of the rule breached, whether the breach was intentional, and excusability. This is the definitive statement of the Francovich conditions and is essential FE-1 reading.

case_name: Dillenkofer v Federal Republic of Germany
citation: Cases C-178/94 and others [1996] ECR I-4845
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Complete failure by a Member State to implement a Directive by the required deadline constitutes per se a sufficiently serious breach for the purpose of State liability, without the need to demonstrate additional factors of manifest and grave disregard. The Francovich conditions are met automatically where there is total non-implementation.
key_quote: Failure to take any measure to transpose a Directive in order to achieve the result it prescribes within the period laid down for that purpose constitutes per se a serious breach of Community law.
full_summary: Germany had failed to implement the Package Travel Directive (90/314/EEC) which required Member States to protect package holiday purchasers from insolvency of tour operators. Dillenkofer and others lost money when their tour operators became insolvent. The ECJ held that complete failure to implement a Directive by the implementation deadline is automatically a sufficiently serious breach — no further analysis of whether the breach was manifest or grave is required. The Directive was intended to confer rights on individuals (condition 1 satisfied), complete non-implementation is per se serious (condition 2 satisfied automatically), and the loss suffered by the plaintiffs was directly caused by the non-implementation (condition 3). Dillenkofer clarifies the Francovich/Brasserie framework for the most common type of breach — non-implementation.

case_name: Köbler v Austria
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: State liability may arise even where the breach of EU law is attributable to the decision of a national court of last resort. Infringement of EU law by a supreme court constitutes a sufficiently serious breach only in exceptional cases, applying a strict test that accounts for the inherent nature of the judicial function.
key_quote: The principle that Member States are obliged to make good damage caused to individuals by infringements of Community law attributable to that State is also applicable where the infringement in question results from a decision of a court adjudicating at last instance.
full_summary: Professor Köbler claimed a long-service increment available to Austrian civil servants with 15 years' service in Austrian universities. He had 15 years' service across Austria and other EU Member States. The Austrian Supreme Administrative Court (Verwaltungsgerichtshof) referred to the ECJ, then withdrew its reference, deciding the case incorrectly contrary to EU free movement law. Köbler sued the Austrian State for the loss. The ECJ held for the first time that a Member State can be liable in Francovich damages for a breach of EU law attributable to its supreme court. This is a significant extension of State liability — courts are not immune. The test for 'sufficiently serious breach' by a court requires manifest infringement of applicable EU law. The case is important FE-1 material on the scope and limits of Francovich liability.

case_name: Becker v Finanzamt Münster-Innenstadt
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Directive provision is directly effective (once the implementation deadline has passed) if it is sufficiently clear, precise, and unconditional so as to create a right which individuals can rely on against the State. The State cannot rely on its own failure to implement to deny an individual the right conferred by the Directive.
key_quote: Wherever the provisions of a Directive appear, as far as their subject-matter is concerned, to be unconditional and sufficiently precise, those provisions may, in the absence of implementing measures adopted within the prescribed period, be relied upon as against any national provision which is incompatible with the Directive.
full_summary: Becker sought to rely on the Sixth VAT Directive (77/388/EEC) which exempted certain credit services from VAT. Germany had not properly implemented the Directive. She argued she could rely on the Directive directly against the German tax authority. The ECJ confirmed the conditions for Directive direct effect: (1) the implementation deadline must have passed; (2) the provision must be sufficiently clear, precise, and unconditional; and (3) it must not leave Member States any discretion. The provision in question was directly effective and Becker could rely on it against the German tax authority. Becker consolidates the conditions for Directive direct effect (building on Van Duyn and Ratti) and is the leading case on the three requirements for Directive direct effect.

case_name: CIA Security International SA v Signalson SA and Securitel SPRL
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Directive provision may be used 'as a shield' in private litigation (invocability of exclusion) even where it cannot impose obligations on private parties. A national measure adopted in breach of the Technical Standards Directive's notification requirements is inapplicable and unenforceable, even in proceedings between private parties.
key_quote: The result of the failure to notify is that the technical regulation is inapplicable and therefore unenforceable as against individuals.
full_summary: CIA Security (a private company) was sued by competitors (Signalson and Securitel) in Belgian courts for marketing alarm systems not approved under Belgian law. CIA Security argued the Belgian approval rules were technical standards that had not been notified to the Commission as required by the Technical Standards Directive (83/189/EEC). The ECJ held that the failure to notify rendered the Belgian rules inapplicable — they could not be enforced against CIA Security even in proceedings between private parties. This is the 'use of Directives as a shield' or 'incidental horizontal effect' doctrine: while a Directive cannot impose direct obligations on private parties, its provisions may exclude the application of national law in private proceedings. CIA Security extends the effective reach of Directives into horizontal disputes in a limited way.

case_name: Procureur du Roi v Benoît and Gustave Dassonville
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: All trading rules enacted by Member States which are capable of hindering, directly or indirectly, actually or potentially, intra-Community trade constitute measures having equivalent effect to a quantitative restriction (MEEQRs) within the meaning of Article 34 TFEU. This is the Dassonville formula — the widest possible definition of an MEEQR.
key_quote: All trading rules enacted by Member States which are capable of hindering, directly or indirectly, actually or potentially, intra-Community trade are to be considered as measures having an effect equivalent to quantitative restrictions.
full_summary: Belgian law required imported goods to have a certificate of authenticity issued by the exporter's national government. Scotch whisky imported from France (where such a certificate was unavailable) could not comply. The ECJ formulated the famously broad 'Dassonville formula': any national measure capable of hindering — even potentially and indirectly — intra-Community trade is a measure having equivalent effect to a quantitative restriction (MEEQR) prohibited by Article 34 TFEU (formerly Article 30 EEC). The breadth of this formula meant that almost any national regulation touching on goods could fall within Article 34. This necessitated the development of justified restrictions (Article 36 TFEU) and the Cassis de Dijon mandatory requirements to manage the enormous potential scope of the rule. Dassonville is the foundational FE-1 case on Article 34 TFEU.

case_name: Rewe-Zentral AG v Bundesmonopolverwaltung für Branntwein (Cassis de Dijon)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Member State may apply its national product requirements to imported goods only where they are necessary to satisfy mandatory requirements of the Member State (such as the effectiveness of fiscal supervision, protection of public health, fairness of commercial transactions, and consumer protection) and are proportionate to the aim. Products lawfully marketed in one Member State must in principle be admitted to any other Member State (the principle of mutual recognition).
key_quote: There is no valid reason why, provided that they have been lawfully produced and marketed in one of the Member States, alcoholic beverages should not be introduced into any other Member State.
full_summary: Rewe sought to import Cassis de Dijon (a French blackcurrant liqueur with 15-20% alcohol) into Germany. German law required that fruit liqueurs have a minimum alcohol content of 25%, which Cassis did not meet. The ECJ held: (1) products lawfully produced and marketed in one Member State must, in principle, be freely admitted to all other Member States (mutual recognition principle); (2) obstacles to free movement arising from national product standards can be justified only by 'mandatory requirements' — overriding public interests such as consumer protection, tax supervision, or public health; (3) the measure must be proportionate to the aim. Germany could not justify its minimum alcohol content rule. Cassis de Dijon introduces both the mutual recognition principle and the mandatory requirements doctrine (now called 'rule of reason'), and is a pivotal FE-1 authority on Article 34 TFEU.

case_name: Criminal Proceedings against Keck and Mithouard
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National provisions that restrict or prohibit certain selling arrangements (as opposed to product requirements) do not constitute measures having equivalent effect to quantitative restrictions (MEEQRs) within the meaning of Article 34 TFEU, provided they apply to all relevant traders operating within the national territory and affect in the same manner, in law and in fact, the marketing of domestic products and those from other Member States.
key_quote: Contrary to what has previously been decided, the application to products from other Member States of national provisions restricting or prohibiting certain selling arrangements is not such as to hinder directly or indirectly, actually or potentially, trade between Member States within the meaning of the Dassonville judgment.
full_summary: Keck and Mithouard resold goods at a price lower than their purchase price, contrary to French law prohibiting resale at a loss. The ECJ held that such provisions — selling arrangement rules — do not fall within Article 34 TFEU even under the broad Dassonville formula. The Court introduced the distinction between 'product requirements' (rules about the nature, composition, packaging, or labelling of goods — which remain caught by Article 34) and 'selling arrangements' (rules about where, when, and how goods may be sold — which are outside Article 34 provided they apply equally in law and fact to domestic and imported products). Keck significantly restricts the scope of Dassonville and is essential FE-1 authority on the limits of Article 34 TFEU. The boundary between product requirements and selling arrangements has proved difficult to apply in practice.

case_name: Commission v Ireland (Buy Irish Campaign)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Government-sponsored campaigns promoting domestic goods at the expense of imported goods constitute measures having equivalent effect to quantitative restrictions (MEEQRs) under Article 34 TFEU, even where the State does not formally regulate or legally require the conduct. A State cannot escape its Treaty obligations by acting through a quasi-independent body.
key_quote: A government campaign to promote domestic goods, even when conducted through a private body, is capable of influencing the behaviour of consumers and thereby restricts the importation of goods from other Member States.
full_summary: The Irish Government organised and funded the 'Buy Irish' campaign through a private body, the Irish Goods Council, to promote Irish goods over imported ones. The Commission challenged this as a violation of Article 34 TFEU. Ireland argued that because the campaign was operated through a non-State body and did not legally require anyone to buy Irish goods, it was not a 'measure' of the State. The ECJ held that Ireland's financial and organisational support for the campaign constituted an MEEQR. The fact that no formal law was enacted was irrelevant — the State's involvement in organising and funding a campaign to influence consumer behaviour to the disadvantage of imported goods was sufficient. This is an important FE-1 Irish authority on the scope of Article 34 TFEU and on what constitutes a 'State measure'.

case_name: Commission v Ireland (Dundalk Water Scheme)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A public procurement specification that requires compliance with a national technical standard (as opposed to an equivalent international or European standard) constitutes a measure having equivalent effect to a quantitative restriction under Article 34 TFEU, even if the specification does not expressly exclude foreign products.
key_quote: The inclusion in the tender specifications of a clause providing that only pipes complying with a national standard constitutes an obstacle to the free movement of goods, as it could be satisfied only by tenderers supplying products manufactured in accordance with Irish standards.
full_summary: Ireland specified in public procurement documents for a water pipeline that pipes had to comply with an Irish standard (IS 188), even though an equivalent international standard existed (ISO 113). A Spanish company whose pipes met the ISO standard could not tender. The ECJ held that insisting on the Irish standard (when an equivalent international standard existed) was an MEEQR contrary to Article 34 TFEU. The fact that the specification was in a public procurement document rather than a law or regulation was irrelevant. This is an important Irish FE-1 case showing how Article 34 applies to public procurement and technical standards.

case_name: R v Henn and Darby
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A prohibition on the import of pornographic material may be justified under Article 36 TFEU (now Article 36 TFEU) on grounds of public morality, provided the prohibition reflects genuinely held standards of public morality in the Member State concerned, even if domestic production of such material is not penalised in the same way.
key_quote: It is for each Member State to determine in accordance with its own scale of values and in the form selected by it the requirements of public morality in its territory.
full_summary: UK customs officers seized pornographic films and magazines imported from the Netherlands. The importers argued this breached Article 34 TFEU. The ECJ held the prohibition was justified under Article 36 TFEU (public morality exception). Each Member State has the right to determine its own standards of public morality. The fact that the UK did not entirely prohibit domestic production or distribution of such material did not prevent it from prohibiting import — the key was whether there was a genuine policy of prohibiting the import of obscene material. Henn and Darby is a leading case on the public morality derogation under Article 36 TFEU and is compared with Conegate (where mere domestic restriction was insufficient to justify an import ban) in FE-1 materials.

case_name: Conegate Ltd v HM Customs and Excise
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Member State cannot rely on the public morality exception under Article 36 TFEU to justify an import ban on goods whose domestic manufacture or sale is lawfully permitted (even if subject to restrictions). If the Member State allows domestic production and sale of the relevant goods, it cannot use a different standard to restrict their import.
key_quote: A Member State may not rely on the public morality exception to prohibit the importation of goods from other Member States when the same goods may be lawfully manufactured and marketed in its own territory.
full_summary: The UK sought to confiscate inflatable dolls and similar articles imported from Germany, claiming they were indecent. In England such articles could be lawfully manufactured and sold (subject to some restrictions) but not imported. The ECJ held the UK could not rely on the public morality exception in Article 36 TFEU. Where a Member State permits domestic manufacture and sale of the goods in question (even if subject to restrictions), it cannot simultaneously argue that the goods are contrary to public morality when imported. The inconsistency between the domestic and import treatment shows there is no genuine public morality policy. Conegate defines the limits of the public morality derogation and is contrasted with Henn and Darby in FE-1 teaching.

case_name: Levin v Staatssecretaris van Justitie
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: For the purpose of EU free movement of workers, the concept of 'worker' must be given a broad EU law meaning. A person who works part-time or earns less than a minimum subsistence level may still be a 'worker' for the purposes of Article 45 TFEU, provided the activity is genuine and effective and not merely marginal and ancillary.
key_quote: The concept of worker must be given a broad interpretation. It covers any person who pursues activities which are real and genuine, to the exclusion of activities on such a small scale as to be regarded as purely marginal and ancillary.
full_summary: Mrs Levin, a British national in the Netherlands, worked part-time as a waitress earning below the Dutch minimum subsistence level. The Netherlands sought to exclude her on the ground she was not a 'worker'. The ECJ held she was a worker. The concept of 'worker' in Article 48 EEC (now Article 45 TFEU) is an EU law concept that cannot be defined more restrictively by Member States. It covers any person who performs real and genuine work, even part-time work earning below a minimum wage, as long as the work is not marginal and ancillary. Member States cannot exclude part-time or low-paid workers from free movement rights. Levin is the foundational FE-1 case on the definition of 'worker' under Article 45 TFEU.

case_name: Lawrie-Blum v Land Baden-Württemberg
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A 'worker' in EU law is a person who: (1) performs services for and under the direction of another; (2) for a certain period of time; (3) in return for remuneration. A trainee teacher who works under the direction of a school and is remunerated (even at a low level) is a worker regardless of national classification.
key_quote: The essential feature of an employment relationship is that for a certain period of time a person performs services for and under the direction of another person in return for which he receives remuneration.
full_summary: Lawrie-Blum was a British national who sought a probationary teaching post in Baden-Württemberg, Germany. She was refused because she was not a German national. Germany argued trainee teachers were in a special public law relationship, not employment. The ECJ held she was a worker within Article 48 EEC (now Article 45 TFEU). The Court defined the concept of worker with the three-limb test: performing services, under the direction of another, for remuneration. The fact that the remuneration was low, or that the relationship was characterised under national law as something other than employment, did not prevent the application of EU free movement law. Lawrie-Blum provides the definitive EU law definition of 'worker' used in FE-1.

case_name: Union Royale Belge des Sociétés de Football Association v Bosman
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Rules preventing workers from freely moving to take up employment in another Member State at the end of their contract constitute unlawful restrictions on free movement of workers under Article 45 TFEU (formerly Article 48 EEC). This applies to rules of private sporting associations as well as national legislative measures.
key_quote: Article 48 [now 45 TFEU] precludes the application of rules laid down by sporting associations under which a professional footballer who is a national of one Member State may, on the expiry of his contract, be prevented from being employed by a club in another Member State.
full_summary: Bosman, a Belgian footballer, was prevented by football transfer rules from joining a French club at the end of his contract because the French club could not afford the transfer fee demanded by his Belgian club. Simultaneously, UEFA rules limited the number of foreign EU players in club squads. The ECJ held both sets of rules breached Article 45 TFEU. First, transfer fee rules constituted a direct obstacle to a worker moving to take up employment on expiry of his contract. Second, nationality quotas in club competitions were nationality-based restrictions on workers. Sports associations must comply with EU free movement law. Bosman is one of the most famous EU law cases and is essential FE-1 authority on the horizontal application of Article 45 to private bodies, and on the meaning of free movement obstacles.

case_name: Trojani v Centre Public d'Aide Sociale de Bruxelles (CPAS)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A person who performs real and genuine work in a rehabilitation programme (even if the work is irregular and the remuneration is low or in-kind) may qualify as a worker under Article 45 TFEU. Where a person is not a worker, they may still have rights as a Union citizen, subject to conditions.
key_quote: It is for the national court to determine whether the activities performed by Mr Trojani are real and genuine in the light of all the circumstances, having regard to the nature of the activities and the relationship in question.
full_summary: Trojani, a French national in Belgium, did 30 hours of work per week in a Salvation Army hostel in exchange for board and a small allowance. Belgium refused him social assistance. The ECJ held that the referring court should determine whether his activities were 'real and genuine' work for the purposes of EU worker status. If Trojani was a worker, he had full Article 45 free movement rights. If not a worker (because the activities were marginal/ancillary), he might still have rights as a Union citizen under Article 21 TFEU (right to move and reside), though subject to conditions about not being an unreasonable burden on the host State. The case is important FE-1 material on the boundary between 'worker' status and Union citizenship rights.

case_name: Van Duyn v Home Office
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Member States may restrict free movement of workers on grounds of public policy, public security, or public health (Article 45(3) TFEU), but restrictions must be based on the personal conduct of the individual concerned. Membership of an organisation may in some circumstances constitute personal conduct justifying a restriction, particularly where the individual's current association is significant.
key_quote: Membership of a body and the activities of the body itself are distinct; but the national authorities are entitled to take into account the individual's association with the body when assessing personal conduct.
full_summary: Ms Van Duyn was refused entry to the UK to work for the Church of Scientology, which the UK Government considered socially harmful. She argued that the Church was not unlawful under UK law and that she could not be restricted simply because of her membership. The ECJ held that Member States retain the right to restrict entry on public policy grounds based on personal conduct, which can include current membership or association with an organisation. The public policy exception can be applied more broadly to nationals of other Member States than to domestic nationals (the Court expressly recognised this asymmetry). The case is one of the most complex FE-1 cases: it covers both Directive direct effect (Van Duyn establishing Directive direct effect for the first time) and the public policy restriction on free movement of workers.

case_name: Carpenter v Secretary of State for the Home Department
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Member State may not deport the third-country national spouse of a service provider (who provides services to recipients in other Member States from his home State) where doing so would interfere with the service provider's right to provide services under Article 56 TFEU, and where the expulsion would breach Article 8 ECHR (right to family life).
key_quote: The separation of Mr and Mrs Carpenter would be detrimental to their family life and, therefore, to the conditions under which Mr Carpenter exercises a fundamental freedom.
full_summary: Mr Carpenter (UK national) ran a business selling advertising space in UK medical journals to advertisers across the EU. His wife (Philippine national) was his carer at home, enabling him to travel to provide services. She faced deportation for overstaying her visa. The ECJ held the UK's expulsion decision breached Article 49 EC (now Article 56 TFEU). Mr Carpenter's ability to provide services across the EU depended on his wife's presence in the UK. Deporting her would interfere with his freedom to provide services. The decision also needed to respect Article 8 ECHR. The case is important for the 'internal situation' doctrine (a person can invoke free movement rights even when providing services from their home State to recipients elsewhere) and for ECHR compliance in free movement cases.

case_name: The Queen v HM Treasury and Commissioners of Inland Revenue, ex parte Daily Mail and General Trust plc
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Companies are creations of national law and do not have inherent rights of mobility under EU law equivalent to those of natural persons. A Member State of incorporation may impose conditions (such as the requirement to obtain prior Treasury consent before transferring the seat of management) on a company wishing to move to another Member State.
key_quote: Unlike natural persons, companies are creatures of the law and, in the present state of Community law, Community law does not preclude Member States from defining the connecting factor required for a company to be entitled to be incorporated under their national law.
full_summary: Daily Mail (a UK company) wished to transfer its management and central control to the Netherlands for tax purposes, but UK law required HM Treasury consent (to protect against tax avoidance). The ECJ held that EU law did not preclude the UK from imposing such a requirement. Companies are creations of national law and the right of establishment does not confer a right to transfer to another Member State while retaining UK company status. The 'exit State' could impose conditions on the departure of a company. Daily Mail was subsequently significantly restricted by Cartesio. It is an important FE-1 case on the limits of EU freedom of establishment for companies and on the 'departure' aspect of company mobility.

case_name: Centros Ltd v Erhvervs- og Selskabsstyrelsen
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Freedom of establishment includes the right to incorporate a company in any Member State and to operate through a branch in another Member State. A Member State may not refuse to register a branch of a company validly incorporated in another Member State merely because the company was formed to avoid its more onerous company law requirements.
key_quote: It is not unlawful to form a company in a Member State whose rules of company law seem less restrictive and to set up branches in other Member States. The Member State hosting the branch may not refuse to register it merely because the company was set up in another Member State to avoid the host State's minimum capital requirements.
full_summary: A British couple formed Centros Ltd in the UK (which had no minimum paid-up capital requirements) to avoid Danish requirements for minimum paid-up capital (DKK 200,000). They then sought to register a branch of Centros in Denmark. Denmark refused, arguing Centros had no real business in the UK and the Danish branch would effectively be the company's principal place of business, circumventing Danish law. The ECJ held the Danish refusal breached Article 43 EC (freedom of establishment). Even if the company was formed to exploit more favourable incorporation conditions in another Member State, that is a legitimate exercise of freedom of establishment. Denmark could not refuse branch registration — it could only address specific fraud risks through proportionate measures. Centros is critical FE-1 authority on company formation shopping within the EU.

case_name: Überseering BV v Nordic Construction Company Baumanagement GmbH
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Member State that applies the real seat doctrine (denying legal personality to foreign companies whose management is located in its territory unless they re-incorporate) violates the freedom of establishment under Articles 49 and 54 TFEU. Member States must recognise the legal capacity of companies validly incorporated in another Member State.
key_quote: Refusing to recognise the legal capacity of a company validly incorporated under the law of another Member State is a blatant restriction on freedom of establishment and is incompatible with Article 43 EC.
full_summary: Überseering was incorporated in the Netherlands. Its shares were acquired by two Germans and its actual management moved to Germany. Under German 'real seat' doctrine, a company whose management is in Germany must be incorporated under German law — Überseering therefore had no legal personality in Germany. German courts rejected Überseering's claim on the ground it had no legal standing. The ECJ held this violated freedom of establishment. A Member State cannot deny legal personality to a company validly incorporated in another EU Member State. The real seat doctrine, as applied to deny legal capacity, is a fundamental restriction on freedom of establishment. Überseering, with Centros and Inspire Art, forms the FE-1 trilogy on company establishment and the mutual recognition of companies.

case_name: Inspire Art Ltd
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Member State cannot impose its own company law requirements (such as minimum capital requirements and disclosure obligations) on a branch of a company incorporated in another Member State, even if the company carries on all its activities through that branch and was formed specifically to avoid the host State's requirements. Such obligations constitute unjustified restrictions on freedom of establishment.
key_quote: The fact that Inspire Art was formed in the United Kingdom for the purpose of circumventing Dutch minimum capital requirements does not deprive it of the benefit of freedom of establishment.
full_summary: Inspire Art was a UK company that operated entirely through a branch in the Netherlands. It was registered in the Netherlands as a 'formally foreign company' under Dutch law, which subjected it to Dutch minimum capital and director liability rules. The ECJ held that Dutch law, insofar as it imposed additional requirements on the branch of a validly incorporated foreign EU company, constituted an unjustified restriction on freedom of establishment. Following Centros and Überseering, the purpose of incorporating in a Member State with more favourable requirements is not an abuse of EU law — it is the legitimate exercise of a fundamental freedom. Inspire Art completes the Centros-Überseering-Inspire Art trilogy, decisively establishing free choice of incorporation law within the EU.

case_name: Gebhard v Consiglio dell'Ordine degli Avvocati e Procuratori di Milano
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National measures liable to hinder or make less attractive the exercise of fundamental Treaty freedoms (establishment, services, workers, capital) must satisfy four conditions (the Gebhard test): (1) applied in a non-discriminatory manner; (2) justified by overriding reasons of general interest; (3) suitable for attaining their objective; and (4) not go beyond what is necessary (proportionality). The test applies across all four freedoms.
key_quote: National measures liable to hinder or make less attractive the exercise of fundamental freedoms must fulfil four conditions: they must be applied in a non-discriminatory manner; they must be justified by imperative requirements in the general interest; they must be suitable for securing attainment of the objective; and they must not go beyond what is necessary.
full_summary: Gebhard, a German lawyer established in Germany, had practised permanently in Milan under the title 'avvocato' (a regulated Italian lawyer title). He was reprimanded by the Milan Bar Council for using the title without being enrolled on the Italian Bar. He argued that, if he was providing temporary services rather than establishing himself, Italian restrictions on using the title could not apply. The ECJ developed the general four-condition test ('Gebhard test') applicable to national measures restricting any of the four fundamental freedoms. The test, derived from mandatory requirements doctrine in goods free movement, generalises the proportionality analysis. The case also clarified the distinction between temporary service provision (Article 56 TFEU) and permanent establishment (Article 49 TFEU). The Gebhard test is a central FE-1 analytical framework across free movement topics.

case_name: Säger v Dennemeyer & Co Ltd
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Article 56 TFEU (free movement of services) precludes national rules that discriminate against service providers from other Member States, but also rules that are applicable to all service providers without distinction but which are liable to prohibit, impede, or render less attractive the provision of services by service providers from other Member States.
key_quote: Article 59 [now 56 TFEU] requires not only the elimination of all discrimination against persons providing services on account of their nationality, but also the abolition of any restriction which is liable to prohibit, impede or render less attractive the activities of a provider of services established in another Member State.
full_summary: Dennemeyer (a UK company) provided patent renewal services to German clients without a licence required under German law. Säger (a German patent agent) complained that this infringed Germany's licensed patent agency monopoly. The ECJ held Germany's licensing requirement constituted a restriction on Article 59 EC (now Article 56 TFEU). Free movement of services is not limited to non-discrimination — it also prohibits national rules that make cross-border service provision less attractive, even where they apply to domestic and foreign providers alike (indistinctly applicable rules). The measure could be justified only by imperative requirements proportionate to the aim. Säger extends the Cassis de Dijon approach (mandatory requirements) from goods to services. It is the foundational FE-1 case on Article 56 TFEU restrictions.

case_name: Alpine Investments BV v Minister van Financiën
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A restriction on cross-border services imposed by the Member State of the service provider (the home State restriction) falls within Article 56 TFEU even though the restriction applies to all providers from that State equally. Article 56 is not limited to restrictions imposed by the host State.
key_quote: Article 59 [now 56 TFEU] covers not only restrictions imposed by the State in which the service recipient is established but also measures taken by the State in which the service provider is established.
full_summary: The Netherlands prohibited 'cold calling' (unsolicited telephone canvassing for financial services) by Dutch financial services firms to potential clients in other Member States. Alpine Investments (a Dutch commodity trader) challenged this as a restriction on its freedom to provide services. The Dutch Government argued Article 56 TFEU only catches restrictions by the host State of the service recipient, not the home State of the service provider. The ECJ held that Article 56 also applies to home State restrictions. A rule preventing a Dutch service provider from offering services to recipients in other EU Member States directly affects access to the market in those States. The prohibition was potentially justifiable (protection of investors) but proportionality had to be assessed. Alpine Investments establishes the FE-1 principle that free movement of services applies symmetrically to both home and host State restrictions.

case_name: Grzelczyk v Centre Public d'Aide Sociale d'Ottignies-Louvain-la-Neuve
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: EU citizenship (Article 20 TFEU) is the fundamental status of nationals of Member States. A Union citizen who is economically inactive may claim social assistance in a host Member State on the same basis as that State's own nationals, relying on the non-discrimination principle (Article 18 TFEU), provided they are not an unreasonable burden on the host State's social assistance system.
key_quote: Union citizenship is destined to be the fundamental status of nationals of the Member States, enabling those who find themselves in the same situation to enjoy the same treatment in law irrespective of their nationality.
full_summary: Grzelczyk, a French national studying in Belgium, applied for a social assistance benefit (minimex) in his final year, having supported himself by working and taking out loans during his first three years. Belgium denied the benefit because he was not Belgian. The ECJ held that Grzelczyk could rely on Article 6 EC (now Article 18 TFEU, non-discrimination on grounds of nationality) and Article 8 EC (now Article 20 TFEU, EU citizenship) to claim the benefit on the same terms as Belgian students. The Court established the famous principle that 'Union citizenship is destined to be the fundamental status of nationals of Member States'. However, the host State may still take into account whether the citizen has become an unreasonable burden. Grzelczyk is the foundational FE-1 case on EU citizenship rights and social benefits.

case_name: Baumbast and R v Secretary of State for the Home Department
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Union citizens have a direct and individual right of residence in another Member State under Article 21 TFEU (formerly Article 18 EC), subject only to the limitations and conditions laid down in the Treaty and secondary legislation. These limitations must be applied in accordance with the principle of proportionality.
key_quote: Article 18(1) EC [now Article 21 TFEU] confers a directly effective right of residence on Union citizens, and any limitations must be applied in conformity with the principle of proportionality.
full_summary: Baumbast, a German national, worked in the UK and then abroad. His UK residence permit expired and the UK refused to renew it because he no longer satisfied the conditions for worker status (he was not employed in the UK). The ECJ held that Baumbast had a directly effective right of residence under Article 18 EC (now Article 21 TFEU) as a Union citizen, independently of any worker status. The right of residence under Article 21 TFEU is directly effective — a Union citizen can invoke it before national courts. The conditions for residence (sufficient resources, health insurance) had been satisfied throughout; any limitations must be proportionately applied. Baumbast is the leading FE-1 authority on the directly effective nature of EU citizenship residence rights.

case_name: Chen v Secretary of State for the Home Department
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A third-country national who is the primary carer of a Union citizen child (with sufficient resources and health insurance) has a derivative right of residence in the Member State where the child resides, as the denial of such a right would deprive the child's citizenship rights of practical effect.
key_quote: Refusal to allow the mother, a third-country national who is the carer of that child, to reside with the child would deprive the child's right of residence of any useful effect.
full_summary: A Chinese national (Mrs Chen) travelled to Northern Ireland to give birth there, so that her child would acquire Irish nationality (under the ius soli rule then in force), and thereby EU citizenship. She then sought to reside in Wales. The UK sought to remove her as she had no right to remain. The ECJ held that the child, as an EU citizen, had a right of residence under Article 18 EC (Baumbast). To ensure the child's right of residence had practical effect, her primary carer (Mrs Chen) must also have a right to reside. Otherwise the child's rights would be meaningless. The 'useful effect' principle requires that derivative rights be recognised for carers of citizen children. Chen is an important FE-1 case on derivative residence rights and the 'useful effect' principle in EU citizenship.

case_name: Ruiz Zambrano v Office National de l'Emploi (ONEm)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Article 20 TFEU (EU citizenship) precludes national measures that have the effect of depriving Union citizens of the genuine enjoyment of the substance of the rights conferred by their status as Union citizens. This applies even in purely internal situations where the citizen has never exercised free movement rights.
key_quote: Article 20 TFEU precludes national measures which have the effect of depriving Union citizens of the genuine enjoyment of the substance of the rights conferred by virtue of their status as Union citizens.
full_summary: Mr and Mrs Ruiz Zambrano were Colombian nationals in Belgium with children born in Belgium who were Belgian nationals (EU citizens). The Belgian authorities rejected their residency claims. If they were expelled from Belgium, their Belgian-national children (who had never moved to another EU Member State and could not exercise free movement rights) would have to leave the EU with them. The ECJ held that Article 20 TFEU precluded this result: depriving the children (Union citizens) of the genuine enjoyment of the substance of their EU citizenship rights (by effectively forcing them to leave the EU) was impermissible. This 'substance of rights' doctrine allows Article 20 TFEU to apply even in purely internal (domestic) situations. Zambrano significantly expanded EU citizenship law and is an important FE-1 case.

case_name: Rottmann v Freistaat Bayern
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A decision by a Member State to withdraw naturalised citizenship from an individual (resulting in the loss of EU citizenship as well) falls within the scope of EU law. Such a decision must be compatible with the principle of proportionality. EU law does not preclude withdrawal of fraudulently obtained nationality, but Member States must have regard to the consequences for the person under EU law.
key_quote: A decision to withdraw naturalisation is not exempt from EU law review merely because it concerns nationality. Member States must, when exercising their powers to define conditions for acquisition and loss of nationality, have due regard to EU law.
full_summary: Rottmann, an Austrian national, was being investigated for serious fraud in Austria. He acquired German nationality through naturalisation (which automatically extinguished his Austrian nationality). Germany then proposed to withdraw his German naturalisation on grounds of fraud, which would have left him stateless and (as stateless) without EU citizenship. The ECJ held that the withdrawal fell within the scope of EU law because it could result in loss of EU citizenship and associated rights. Although Member States determine nationality, they must exercise that power in conformity with EU law, including proportionality. Rottmann is a significant FE-1 case establishing that EU law constrains Member States' nationality decisions where EU citizenship is at stake.

case_name: Établissements Consten SàRL and Grundig-Verkaufs-GmbH v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: An agreement between undertakings that partitions the Common Market (by allocating exclusive territories and preventing parallel imports) restricts competition and breaches Article 101 TFEU (formerly Article 85 EEC), even if it may increase intra-brand competition. The EU competition rules serve the single market objective of preventing market partitioning along national lines.
key_quote: The principle of freedom of competition concerns the various stages and manifestations of competition. Although competition between producers is generally more noticeable than that between distributors... it does not thereby follow that an agreement tending to restrict the latter kind of competition should escape the prohibition in Article 85(1).
full_summary: Grundig (German manufacturer) appointed Consten as its exclusive distributor in France, with absolute territorial protection preventing parallel imports and all resellers from selling Grundig products outside their territories. UNEF (a French trader) bought Grundig products cheaply in Germany and sold them in France in competition with Consten. Consten relied on GINT trademark rights (assigned by Grundig) to stop UNEF. The Commission held the agreement breached Article 85 EEC. The Court upheld the decision: agreements partitioning the market along national lines are contrary to the fundamental objective of establishing a single market. Even if the agreement increased intra-brand competition, the elimination of parallel imports violated the Treaty. Consten and Grundig is a seminal FE-1 competition case on the relationship between competition law and market integration.

case_name: Wouters v Algemene Raad van de Nederlandse Orde van Advocaten
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A decision of a professional body (such as a Bar Council) that restricts competition may not breach Article 101 TFEU where, taking into account its context, objectives, and the legal framework within which it operates, the decision is necessary for the proper practice of the profession and the restriction is inherent in the pursuit of a legitimate objective. This is the Wouters 'rule of reason' or 'inherent restriction' doctrine.
key_quote: Not every agreement between undertakings which restricts the freedom of action of the parties or of one of them necessarily falls within the prohibition laid down in Article 81(1) [now 101 TFEU].
full_summary: The Dutch Bar Council's regulation prohibited multi-disciplinary partnerships between lawyers and accountants. Wouters wished to enter into such a partnership and challenged the rule. The ECJ held the regulation did restrict competition within Article 81 EC (now Article 101 TFEU) — it prevented collaboration between two professions. However, applying the 'rule of reason', the regulation fell outside Article 101(1): the Dutch Bar Council had a legitimate aim (maintaining the independence and ethics of the legal profession) and the restriction was necessary and proportionate to that aim. The 'Wouters exception' allows restrictions on competition that are inherent in and proportionate to a legitimate regulatory objective to escape Article 101(1). This is important FE-1 material on the scope of Article 101 TFEU.

case_name: Meca-Medina and Majcen v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Anti-doping rules adopted by sports bodies constitute decisions by associations of undertakings under Article 101 TFEU. They are not automatically exempt merely because of sport's special character. However, if the rules pursue a legitimate sporting objective (ensuring fair competition and athletes' health) and the restrictions are inherent and proportionate, they fall outside Article 101(1) under the Wouters doctrine.
key_quote: Anti-doping rules are capable of falling within the scope of Article 81 EC [now Article 101 TFEU]. Even so, not every rule automatically restricts competition; the nature, general context, objectives, and proportionality of the rule must be assessed.
full_summary: Two swimmers were disqualified and banned by the International Olympic Committee/FINA for doping. They challenged the anti-doping rules as violating competition law. The ECJ held that the doping rules were decisions of an association of undertakings within Article 81 EC (now Article 101 TFEU).
