The mere sporting character of the rules did not exempt them from competition law. However, applying the Wouters analysis, the anti-doping rules pursued a legitimate aim (fair competition, athletes' health) and the restrictions imposed were inherent and proportionate. Therefore the rules did not infringe Article 101 TFEU. Meca-Medina overturned the earlier 'purely sporting' exemption and establishes the general applicability of EU competition law to sport, moderated by the Wouters exception.

case_name: United Brands Co and United Brands Continentaal BV v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A dominant position under Article 102 TFEU exists where an undertaking has the power to behave to an appreciable extent independently of its competitors, customers, and consumers. Dominance is assessed on the relevant product market and geographic market. A very large market share is an important but not conclusive indicator of dominance.
key_quote: The dominant position referred to in this Article relates to a position of economic strength enjoyed by an undertaking which enables it to prevent effective competition being maintained on the relevant market by giving it the power to behave to an appreciable extent independently of its competitors, customers and ultimately of its consumers.
full_summary: United Brands (UB), the world's largest banana distributor, was accused of abusing a dominant position in the banana market. The ECJ defined the relevant market as the banana market (not the wider fresh fruit market), holding that bananas were a separate market for certain consumer segments. It defined dominance as the power to behave independently of competitors and customers. UB held very large market shares (40-45%) across the relevant geographic markets. Abuses found included: charging unfair prices in some Member States, refusing to supply a distributor without objective justification, and imposing discriminatory prices. United Brands is the foundational FE-1 case on the definition of dominance, relevant market definition, and examples of abusive conduct under Article 102 TFEU.

case_name: Hoffmann-La Roche & Co AG v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Fidelity rebates (discounts conditional on the customer purchasing all or most of their requirements from the dominant firm) constitute an abuse of a dominant position under Article 102 TFEU, even if they do not directly harm consumers. The abuse consists of loyalty obligations that impede competitors from accessing customers.
key_quote: An undertaking which is in a dominant position on a market and ties purchasers — even if it does so at their request — by an obligation to obtain all or most of their requirements exclusively from the said undertaking commits an abuse within the meaning of Article 86 [now 102 TFEU].
full_summary: Hoffmann-La Roche supplied vitamin products to large industrial purchasers under agreements that gave discounts (fidelity rebates) to buyers who obtained most of their requirements from HLR. The Commission found this constituted an abuse of dominant position. The ECJ upheld the finding. Fidelity rebates that tie buyers to purchase all or most of their requirements from the dominant firm are inherently abusive — they foreclose competitors from accessing those customers and reinforce the dominant firm's market power. Unlike ordinary quantity rebates (which reflect efficiencies), fidelity rebates serve no efficiency purpose beyond locking in customers. HLR is the leading FE-1 authority on fidelity rebates and exclusionary abuse under Article 102 TFEU.

case_name: Commercial Solvents Corporation v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A dominant undertaking that holds a monopoly in the supply of a raw material commits an abuse of dominant position under Article 102 TFEU if it refuses to continue supplying a downstream customer in order to eliminate that customer as a competitor in a derived market. Refusal to supply by a dominant firm that eliminates competition on a downstream market is abusive.
key_quote: An undertaking in a dominant position cannot, simply to strengthen its position on the market, eliminate a competitor by ceasing to supply raw materials to that competitor.
full_summary: Commercial Solvents (CSC) monopolised the supply of a raw material (aminobutanol) used to produce a drug (ethambutol). CSC had supplied Zoja (an Italian company) with the raw material. CSC then decided to produce ethambutol itself and refused to supply Zoja with aminobutanol, effectively eliminating Zoja as a competitor. The ECJ upheld the Commission's finding of abuse: an undertaking dominant on the market for a raw material may not cease supplying a customer in order to eliminate that customer as a competitor in a downstream market where the dominant firm also operates. Commercial Solvents is the foundational FE-1 case on refusal to supply as an abuse of dominant position and on leveraging dominance from one market to another.

case_name: Magill TV Guide / ITP, BBC and RTE v Commission
citation: Cases C-241/91 P and C-242/91 P [1995] ECR I-743
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: In exceptional circumstances, a refusal by a dominant undertaking to license intellectual property rights may constitute an abuse of a dominant position under Article 102 TFEU. Such circumstances include: the refusal prevents the emergence of a new product for which there is consumer demand; there is no justification; and the dominant firm is reserving a secondary market to itself.
key_quote: The exercise of an exclusive right by the proprietor may involve abusive conduct under Article 86 [now 102] in exceptional circumstances, particularly where the exercise prevents the appearance of a new product which the proprietor does not offer.
full_summary: RTE, BBC, and ITP held copyright in their weekly TV programme listings and refused to licence them to Magill, a publisher seeking to produce a comprehensive weekly TV guide (which none of the broadcasters offered). The ECJ upheld the Commission's finding of abuse. In the exceptional circumstances — the refusal prevented the emergence of a new product (a comprehensive weekly guide) for which there was strong consumer demand; there was no objective justification for the refusal; and the broadcasters were reserving to themselves the secondary market for comprehensive TV guides — the refusal to licence amounted to an abuse of dominant position. Magill is the leading case on compulsory licensing under Article 102 TFEU and the 'exceptional circumstances' test. The Irish context makes it particularly relevant for FE-1.

case_name: Stauder v City of Ulm
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Fundamental human rights are enshrined in the general principles of Community (EU) law and protected by the Court of Justice. Community law must be interpreted in a way that respects the fundamental rights of individuals.
key_quote: Interpreted in this way, the provision at issue contains nothing capable of prejudicing the fundamental human rights enshrined in the general principles of Community law and protected by the Court.
full_summary: A Community Decision required Member States selling butter at reduced prices to welfare recipients to identify recipients by name on vouchers. Stauder challenged the German implementation, arguing the requirement to reveal his name violated his dignity. The ECJ interpreted the Decision as not requiring name identification (an interpretation consistent with fundamental rights), and added the statement that fundamental human rights are enshrined in the general principles of Community law and protected by the Court. Stauder is the seed case — the first time the ECJ acknowledged fundamental rights as part of Community law. This was subsequently developed in Internationale Handelsgesellschaft (constitutional traditions as a source) and Nold (international human rights conventions as a source). For FE-1, Stauder is the starting point for the evolution of EU fundamental rights law.

case_name: Nold v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Fundamental rights form an integral part of the general principles of EU law. The Court draws on the common constitutional traditions of Member States and international human rights treaties ratified by Member States (in particular the ECHR) as sources of inspiration for EU fundamental rights, even if those instruments are not directly applicable as part of Community law.
key_quote: In safeguarding these rights, the Court is bound to draw inspiration from constitutional traditions common to the Member States and from international human rights treaties on which Member States have collaborated.
full_summary: Nold, a German coal wholesaler, argued that a Commission Decision approving new ECSC trading conditions infringed his property rights and right to pursue a trade or profession. The ECJ rejected his claim on the merits but importantly developed the sources of EU fundamental rights: (1) common constitutional traditions of Member States; (2) international treaties on human rights to which Member States are parties (especially the ECHR). The Court would not uphold measures that violated these rights. Nold built on Stauder and Internationale Handelsgesellschaft to create the foundational sources of EU fundamental rights law, ultimately leading to the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (2000/2009). This is essential FE-1 material on the historical development of EU fundamental rights.

case_name: Wachauf v Bundesamt für Ernährung und Forstwirtschaft
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: EU fundamental rights bind Member States when they implement or apply EU law. Where national authorities are acting within the scope of EU law (e.g., implementing an EU regulation), their actions must comply with fundamental rights as general principles of EU law.
key_quote: The requirements flowing from the protection of fundamental rights in the Community legal order are also binding on Member States when they implement Community rules.
full_summary: Wachauf was a tenant farmer who built up a milk production business. When his lease ended, he sought compensation under an EU milk production scheme. The national authority refused. He argued the refusal to transfer reference quantities deprived him of his property. The ECJ held that national authorities implementing EU regulations must comply with EU fundamental rights. The Member State's discretion in implementing EU rules must be exercised in a way that respects fundamental rights (here, property rights). Wachauf establishes the principle that EU fundamental rights bind Member States when they act within the scope of EU law. Contrasted with ERT (where fundamental rights constrain Member States relying on Treaty derogations from EU law). This distinction is central to FE-1 fundamental rights analysis.

case_name: Elliniki Radiophonia Tileórassi AE v Dimotiki Etairia Pliroforissis (ERT)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: When a Member State relies on a Treaty derogation to justify a restriction on a fundamental EU freedom, any national measures adopted must comply with EU fundamental rights as general principles of EU law. The use of Treaty derogations brings national measures within the scope of EU law.
key_quote: When a Member State relies on provisions of the Treaty in order to justify national legislation which is liable to obstruct the exercise of a fundamental freedom, such justification must be interpreted in the light of the general principles of law and in particular fundamental rights.
full_summary: ERT held a Greek state television monopoly, relying on the public security exception in Article 66 EC (now Article 52 TFEU) for broadcasting. A competitor challenged the monopoly. The ECJ held that where a Member State invokes a Treaty derogation (such as the public policy/security exceptions) to restrict a fundamental freedom, the measures taken must comply with EU fundamental rights. This is because the use of a Treaty-sanctioned derogation brings the national measure within the scope of EU law. ERT extends the Wachauf principle: EU fundamental rights apply to Member States not only when they implement EU rules (Wachauf) but also when they invoke Treaty derogations (ERT). These two cases together define the scope of the EU fundamental rights obligation on Member States for FE-1.

case_name: Plaumann & Co v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: For an individual (non-privileged applicant) to challenge an EU act under Article 263 TFEU (formerly Article 173 EEC), the applicant must be 'individually concerned' by the act. An applicant is individually concerned only if the act affects them by reason of attributes peculiar to them, or by reason of circumstances distinguishing them from all other persons. Membership of an open category does not satisfy this test.
key_quote: Persons other than those to whom a Decision is addressed may only claim to be individually concerned if that decision affects them by reason of certain attributes which are peculiar to them or by reason of circumstances in which they are differentiated from all other persons.
full_summary: Plaumann (a German clementine importer) sought to annul a Commission Decision refusing Germany authorisation to reduce customs duties on clementines. Plaumann argued it was individually concerned as an importer. The ECJ held Plaumann could not show individual concern. A person importing clementines belongs to an open and non-defined category — anyone could in theory import clementines and thereby be affected in the same way. Plaumann's position was not differentiated from all other potential importers. The Plaumann test has been notoriously restrictive, making it very difficult for private individuals to directly challenge EU legislative acts under Article 263. The test is still in force (UPA confirmed it could not be overruled without Treaty amendment) and is the central FE-1 case on standing to challenge EU acts.

case_name: Union de Pequeños Agricultores (UPA) v Council
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The restrictive Plaumann test of individual concern for direct challenges under Article 263 TFEU cannot be relaxed by the Court of Justice — only a Treaty amendment could do so. The right to challenge EU acts indirectly (through preliminary reference under Article 267 TFEU) and to seek State liability for invalid acts ensures effective judicial protection without the need to weaken the Plaumann test.
key_quote: It is for the Member States to establish a system of legal remedies and procedures which ensure respect for the fundamental right to effective judicial protection... the Court cannot, without exceeding its jurisdiction, interpret the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC in a way that derogates from the conditions it expressly lays down.
full_summary: UPA (a Spanish agricultural association) sought to challenge an EU regulation on olive oil subsidies, arguing the Plaumann test denied effective judicial protection to private applicants. The ECJ (Grand Chamber) held that it could not broaden the conditions for admissibility in Article 230(4) EC (now Article 263(4) TFEU) without amending the Treaty. The obligation to ensure effective judicial protection fell on Member States — national courts must allow individuals to raise invalidity through the preliminary reference procedure. Advocate General Jacobs gave a famous opinion proposing liberalisation (the 'substantial adverse effect' test), which was not followed. UPA reaffirmed Plaumann and shifted the burden to national systems and Article 267 TFEU to ensure access to justice. The case is critical FE-1 material on the limits of private party standing under Article 263.

case_name: Parti écologiste 'Les Verts' v European Parliament
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The EU (then EEC) constitutes a 'Community based on the rule of law' in which all acts of the institutions, including those of the Parliament, are subject to judicial review. Acts of the European Parliament that produce legal effects vis-à-vis third parties are reviewable under Article 263 TFEU (formerly Article 173 EEC) even though Parliament was not originally listed as a reviewable institution.
key_quote: The European Economic Community is a Community based on the rule of law, inasmuch as neither its Member States nor its institutions can avoid a review of the question whether the measures adopted by them are in conformity with the basic constitutional charter, the Treaty.
full_summary: Les Verts (the French Green Party) sought to annul a Parliament decision on reimbursement of electoral campaign expenses, which allegedly favoured established parties. The Parliament argued its acts could not be challenged as it was not listed in Article 173 EEC as a reviewable institution. The ECJ held Parliament's measures with legal effects vis-à-vis third parties were subject to review, notwithstanding the literal text of Article 173. The EEC is based on the rule of law — all institutions must comply with the Treaty and acts producing legal effects must be subject to judicial scrutiny. The Court interpreted Article 173 expansively to include Parliament. Les Verts is the key FE-1 case on the EU as a Community/Union based on the rule of law and on the principle of complete judicial protection.

case_name: CILFIT Srl and Lanificio di Gavardo SpA v Ministry of Health
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Courts of last instance are obliged to make a preliminary reference under Article 267 TFEU unless: (1) the question is irrelevant to the outcome (CILFIT condition 1); (2) the question has already been answered by the Court (acte éclairé — CILFIT condition 2); or (3) the answer is so obvious as to leave no room for reasonable doubt (acte clair — CILFIT condition 3). These are the CILFIT conditions for the exception to the obligation to refer.
key_quote: The obligation to refer may be waived where the question raised is irrelevant, where the matter is already settled, or where the correct application of Community law is so obvious as to leave no scope for any reasonable doubt.
full_summary: An Italian company contested a veterinary inspection fee. The Italian court of last instance asked whether it was obliged to refer EU law questions to the ECJ. The ECJ clarified the scope of the Article 177 EEC (now Article 267 TFEU) obligation on courts of last instance. Lower courts may (but need not) refer; courts of last instance must refer unless one of the CILFIT conditions is satisfied. The acte clair condition requires the national court to be convinced that the answer is equally obvious to courts of other Member States, considering EU law's multilingual nature and the different methods of interpretation used in EU law. The CILFIT conditions are a central FE-1 topic and define the circumstances in which national courts (particularly the Irish Supreme Court and Court of Appeal) are or are not required to make preliminary references.

case_name: Foto-Frost v Hauptzollamt Lübeck-Ost
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National courts may not themselves declare EU acts invalid; only the Court of Justice has jurisdiction to declare an EU act void. A national court that has doubts about the validity of an EU act must suspend the proceedings and make a preliminary reference to the ECJ under Article 267 TFEU.
key_quote: National courts do not have the power to declare acts of Community institutions invalid. The Court of Justice alone has jurisdiction to declare such acts void.
full_summary: Foto-Frost challenged the validity of a Commission Decision before a German customs court. The German court considered the Decision might be invalid and wondered whether it could declare it so itself or had to refer. The ECJ held that national courts have no power to declare EU acts invalid. Since decisions of validity must be uniform throughout the EU (an EU act cannot be valid in some Member States and invalid in others), only the ECJ can make such declarations. A national court that entertains doubts about validity must refer to the ECJ and, if urgently necessary, may grant interim suspension. Foto-Frost establishes the monopoly of the ECJ on declarations of invalidity of EU acts and is a fundamental FE-1 case on the relationship between national courts and the preliminary reference procedure.

case_name: Rheinmühlen-Düsseldorf v Einfuhr- und Vorratsstelle für Getreide und Futtermittel (No 1)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A national court that is bound by the ruling of a superior court under national law may nonetheless make a preliminary reference to the ECJ under Article 267 TFEU if it has doubts about the compatibility of the superior court's ruling with EU law. National rules binding lower courts to the decisions of higher courts cannot prevent a lower court from referring to the ECJ.
key_quote: The lower court must be able to refer the question to the Court of Justice if it considers that the higher court's ruling could lead it to give a judgment contrary to Community law.
full_summary: A lower German court (Finanzgericht) referred questions to the ECJ relating to a matter on which the higher court (Bundesfinanzhof) had already given a ruling binding on the lower court under German law. The ECJ held that any national court may refer questions of EU law to the ECJ, regardless of national rules requiring lower courts to follow superior courts' decisions. If a lower court believes the superior court's decision involves an incompatibility with EU law, it must be free to refer. EU law's uniformity requires that any court, not only the final court, can refer. Rheinmühlen is an important FE-1 case on the width of the right (not just the obligation) to refer under Article 267 TFEU.

case_name: SPIEGEL Online GmbH v Beck
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The CILFIT acte clair condition requires that a court of last instance be convinced that the answer to a question of EU law is equally obvious to the courts of the other Member States and to the CJEU. The condition must be applied rigorously and cannot be satisfied merely because the outcome seems clear to the referring court itself.
key_quote: The possibility of applying acte clair is limited and must be applied with great caution. The national court must be convinced that the matter is equally obvious to the courts of the other Member States.
full_summary: A German copyright dispute led to questions about the quotation right in the Copyright Directive (2001/29/EC). The German Federal Court of Justice referred preliminary questions, clarifying the application of the CILFIT doctrine. The ECJ (Grand Chamber) refined the acte clair condition: a court of last instance can only refrain from referring if it is 'beyond reasonable doubt' how the question of EU law should be resolved, having regard to the characteristics of EU law (linguistic diversity, autonomous interpretation methods, the possibility of existing CJEU case law). The decision refined CILFIT for the modern era and is important FE-1 material on the current application of the preliminary reference obligation.

case_name: Internationale Handelsgesellschaft mbH v Einfuhr- und Vorratsstelle für Getreide (Solange I context)
court: Federal Constitutional Court (Germany)
legal_principle: National constitutional courts retain the right to review EU acts for compliance with national fundamental rights guarantees so long as ('Solange') EU law does not provide adequate fundamental rights protection equivalent to national constitutional standards. This created the 'Solange' controversy about the relationship between national constitutional courts and the supremacy of EU law.
key_quote: So long as the integration process has not progressed so far that Community law has a codified catalogue of fundamental rights decided on by a parliament and adequate in comparison with the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Basic Law, a court may be permitted to submit Community law to national constitutional scrutiny.
full_summary: The German Federal Constitutional Court (BVerfG) considered the ECJ's ruling in Internationale Handelsgesellschaft (that EU law supremacy extends even over national constitutions) and declined to accept it without qualification. The BVerfG held that it reserved the right to review EU acts for compliance with German fundamental rights guarantees 'so long as' EU law itself did not provide equivalent protection. This created the famous 'Solange' (German for 'as long as') controversy. The stand-off was resolved in Solange II (1986) — once the ECJ developed its fundamental rights jurisprudence to an adequate standard, the BVerfG withdrew its reservation. This inter-court dialogue ultimately contributed to the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights. For FE-1, the Solange controversy is essential context for understanding the development of EU fundamental rights and the tensions between EU supremacy and national constitutional identity.

case_name: Humblot v Directeur des Services Fiscaux
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A national tax system that imposes special surtax on high-powered imported cars while domestically produced cars fall below the tax threshold constitutes discriminatory taxation prohibited by Article 110 TFEU. The discriminatory effect need not be intentional; it suffices that the tax system in practice disadvantages imports. Member States retain autonomy in tax policy only where that policy does not discriminate against goods from other Member States.
key_quote: The special tax involves discrimination against imported cars which is prohibited by Article [110 TFEU].
full_summary: France operated a two-tier vehicle tax: a standard annual road tax and a special surtax applying only to cars above a certain horsepower rating. No French-manufactured cars reached the surtax threshold, but imported cars—including Mr Humblot's Mercedes—did. The CJEU found that, although the tax was facially neutral (applying to all high-powered cars regardless of origin), it produced a discriminatory effect in practice because domestic producers did not manufacture vehicles in the surtax category. This amounted to a protective effect in breach of Article 110 TFEU (then Art 95 EEC). The case is important for FE-1 as it demonstrates the breadth of Article 110: discriminatory taxation includes not only formally discriminatory laws but also facially neutral measures that in practice burden imports while shielding domestic products. It also illustrates the complementary relationship between Article 110 (taxation) and Article 34 (quantitative restrictions): the two overlap to ensure free movement of goods is not circumvented through fiscal means.

case_name: Sandoz BV v Hoofdproduktschap voor Akkerbouwprodukten
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A national requirement of prior authorisation before marketing a foodstuff containing added vitamins constitutes a measure having equivalent effect to a quantitative restriction under Article 34 TFEU. Such a measure may be justified under Article 36 on grounds of public health, but only if the Member State can demonstrate a genuine health risk that is not already addressed by harmonisation measures and the restriction is proportionate.
key_quote: A prior authorisation procedure for the marketing of foodstuffs containing added vitamins is a measure having equivalent effect to a quantitative restriction.
full_summary: The Netherlands required prior ministerial authorisation before any foodstuff with added vitamins could be marketed. Sandoz imported vitamin-enriched muesli from Germany, where it was lawfully sold. The Dutch authorities refused authorisation without giving detailed reasons. The CJEU held that the prior authorisation requirement constituted an MEQR under Article 34 TFEU. It could potentially be justified under Article 36 on public health grounds, because excess vitamin intake can be harmful; however, justification requires the Member State to show that the measure is proportionate and that there is a genuine risk not adequately managed at EU level. Blanket prior authorisation without clear criteria or a transparent procedure went further than necessary. The case is significant for the FE-1 because it illustrates: (1) the wide Dassonville formula capturing prior authorisation requirements; (2) the public health derogation under Article 36; (3) the proportionality requirement limiting derogations; and (4) the interaction of free movement rules with food law harmonisation.

case_name: Commission v Italy (Statistical Levy)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Any pecuniary charge, however small, imposed on goods by reason of the fact that they cross a frontier constitutes a charge having equivalent effect to a customs duty (CEE) and is prohibited by Articles 28–30 TFEU, irrespective of whether it is discriminatory, its purpose, or the designation given to it by national law. A charge for a service actually rendered to the importer may escape classification as a CEE only if it is commensurate with the service and that service is compulsory and of direct benefit to the trader.
key_quote: Any pecuniary charge, whatever its designation and mode of application, which is imposed unilaterally on domestic or foreign goods by reason of the fact that they cross a frontier… constitutes a charge having equivalent effect.
full_summary: Italy levied a small statistical charge on all goods crossing its borders, justified as funding a statistical service beneficial to traders and the economy. The Commission brought infringement proceedings arguing this amounted to a charge having equivalent effect (CEE) to a customs duty. The CJEU held that the charge was indeed a CEE: the prohibition on customs duties and CEEs applies to all charges on goods crossing borders, regardless of the amount, purpose, or label applied to the charge. The statistical benefit to the economy did not constitute a service rendered directly to the trader obliged to pay; it was a general advantage. This case established the broad definition of CEE that has endured. It is foundational for FE-1 in distinguishing charges (prohibited) from internal taxes (governed by Art 110) and genuine fees for services (permitted if proportionate and directly benefiting the payer). The case also demonstrates the Commission's role in enforcement through Article 258 TFEU proceedings.

case_name: Commission v Germany (Beer Purity Law)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National legislation prohibiting the marketing of beer containing additives, while permitting such additives in other beverages, constitutes an MEQR incompatible with Article 34 TFEU. The justification of consumer protection may be achieved by labelling rather than by an outright marketing ban, and a blanket prohibition on additives that are permitted in other Member States is disproportionate.
key_quote: The Beer Purity Law goes beyond what is necessary to satisfy the requirements of consumer protection and cannot be justified under Article [36 TFEU].
full_summary: Germany's Reinheitsgebot (Beer Purity Law) prohibited the sale of beer manufactured with any additives, including those permitted in other EU Member States. Imported beers made with additives lawful elsewhere could not be marketed in Germany as 'beer.' The Commission brought infringement proceedings. The CJEU found the law constituted an MEQR. Germany argued justification on public health (additives may be harmful) and consumer protection (German consumers identify 'beer' as pure). The Court rejected both. On public health, Germany had not established that the permitted additives posed genuine risk and other beverages were permitted to contain them. On consumer protection, appropriate labelling was sufficient to inform consumers of the composition, making an import ban disproportionate. The case is important for FE-1 as a classic illustration of proportionality review of Article 36 derogations and Cassis de Dijon mandatory requirements, and as a reminder that 'mandatory requirements' cannot justify absolute import bans.

case_name: Commission v Denmark (Danish Bottles)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National rules requiring beverages to be marketed only in approved reusable containers constitute an MEQR under Article 34 TFEU, but may be justified on environmental protection grounds as a mandatory requirement under the Cassis de Dijon doctrine. However, any such system must be proportionate; a requirement that all containers be approved from a limited pre-approved list, without exception, goes beyond what is necessary and therefore fails proportionality.
key_quote: The protection of the environment is a mandatory requirement which may limit the application of Article [34 TFEU].
full_summary: Denmark introduced a system requiring beer and soft drinks to be sold only in approved reusable containers with a deposit-and-return scheme. Non-Danish producers found it commercially unviable to invest in complying with the Danish system. The CJEU confirmed that environmental protection could constitute a mandatory requirement justifying an MEQR under the rule of reason introduced in Cassis de Dijon. This was a significant expansion, as Article 36 TFEU does not expressly list environmental protection among its derogations. However, the specific feature of the Danish scheme—requiring containers to be from a pre-approved list—was disproportionate; a less restrictive requirement allowing non-approved containers subject to a quantity limit would suffice. The case is significant for FE-1 as the first CJEU decision recognising environmental protection as a mandatory requirement justifying trade restrictions, and for illustrating that even legitimate environmental objectives are subject to proportionality.

case_name: Commission v France (Spanish Strawberries)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Member State has an obligation under Article 34 TFEU read in conjunction with Article 4(3) TEU (duty of sincere cooperation) to take all necessary measures to ensure free movement of goods on its territory, including by preventing private parties from obstructing imports. Failure to take adequate steps to stop sustained private action impeding imports constitutes a breach of EU law attributable to the Member State.
key_quote: A Member State is obliged to take all necessary and proportionate measures in order to guarantee the full scope of that freedom within its territory.
full_summary: French farmers repeatedly destroyed lorry-loads of Spanish strawberries and other products in protest at competition from Spanish imports, and the French authorities consistently declined to intervene meaningfully. The Commission brought Article 258 proceedings against France. The CJEU confirmed that Article 34 TFEU imposes positive obligations on Member States. While private individuals cannot breach Article 34 (which is addressed to Member States), a Member State that systematically fails to protect free movement against private obstruction itself infringes EU law. France's passivity—stemming from political pressure—could not be justified by public order concerns, as the response was disproportionately inadequate. The case is important for FE-1 as it shows that the free movement rules impose positive obligations on Member States to actively ensure the internal market functions, extending beyond merely refraining from introducing trade barriers.

case_name: PreussenElektra AG v Schhleswag AG
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National legislation requiring private electricity supply undertakings to purchase electricity from renewable energy sources at fixed minimum prices above market value does not constitute State aid under Article 107 TFEU where no state resources are transferred, even though the obligation is imposed by law. Such legislation may, however, constitute an MEQR under Article 34 TFEU if it effectively restricts imports of electricity from other Member States.
key_quote: The obligation imposed on private electricity supply undertakings … does not involve any direct or indirect transfer of State resources.
full_summary: German law required regional electricity supply companies to purchase renewable electricity from producers in their region at guaranteed minimum prices. This favoured domestic green electricity producers. PreussenElektra challenged this scheme as State aid and as an MEQR. On State aid: the CJEU held there was no State aid because no State resources were transferred—the costs fell on private undertakings. On free movement of goods: the scheme did restrict trade in electricity (an Article 34 good) because it created a preference for domestically produced renewable electricity. However, the CJEU found it could be justified on environmental protection grounds—a mandatory requirement—and was considered proportionate at the time, given that harmonisation of the electricity market was incomplete. The case is significant for both the State aid and free movement of goods analyses, particularly in delineating what counts as 'State resources' and confirming environmental protection as a Cassis mandatory requirement.

case_name: Commission v Ireland (Irish Souvenirs)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National legislation requiring imported souvenirs to bear an indication of their place of manufacture unless they display a characteristic Irish symbol (shamrock, wolfhound, round tower) constitutes an MEQR under Article 34 TFEU. Consumer protection or the protection of industrial and commercial property cannot justify measures that have a systematically protectionist effect by favouring products bearing national symbols.
key_quote: The disputed measures … are not measures falling within the exceptions in Article [36 TFEU] since they are not justified on grounds of the protection of industrial and commercial property or the protection of consumers.
full_summary: Ireland enacted regulations requiring souvenirs sold as Irish to display a 'made in Ireland' or country-of-origin marking, unless they depicted traditional Irish motifs. In practice, imported souvenirs with Irish motifs could be sold freely, but generic imports without Irish symbols had to be marked with their actual origin. The Commission argued this was an MEQR. Ireland pleaded consumer protection and protection of industrial and commercial property as justifications. The CJEU held the measure was an MEQR as it discriminated against generic imports. Consumer protection could not justify the measure because Irish motifs were not protected designs—anyone could place a shamrock on any product. The case is instructive for FE-1 on the limits of Article 36 derogations: they must be interpreted strictly, cannot be used protectively, and require genuine justification rather than post-hoc rationalisation.

case_name: R v Immigration Appeal Tribunal, ex parte Antonissen
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: EU nationals have the right to enter another Member State to seek employment for a reasonable period, and cannot be deported during that time provided they are genuinely seeking work and have a reasonable prospect of being engaged. A six-month period for job-seeking is a legitimate minimum, but Member States may deport a job-seeker only after a reasonable period has elapsed and the individual has no genuine prospect of employment.
key_quote: Community nationals … have the right to move freely within the territory of the Member States in order to seek employment there.
full_summary: Antonissen, a Belgian national, entered the UK and was unable to find employment. After six months the UK sought to deport him under immigration rules providing that an EEC national who had not found work within six months could be removed. He argued that EU law gave him the right to remain to seek work. The CJEU confirmed that the right to free movement of workers encompasses the right to enter another Member State for the purpose of seeking employment, even in the absence of a concrete job offer. A six-month period is a reasonable minimum during which deportation is impermissible, but this period may be extended if the individual provides evidence of continuing job-seeking with genuine prospects. The decision is important for FE-1 in establishing that the right to seek work is an implied component of Article 45 TFEU (free movement of workers) and for demonstrating the Court's purposive interpretation of Treaty provisions to give maximum effect to free movement rights.

case_name: Bettray v Staatssecretaris van Justitie
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Work performed as part of a drug rehabilitation programme, which is not genuine and effective economic activity but is merely a means of rehabilitating or reintegrating the person concerned, does not satisfy the definition of 'worker' under Article 45 TFEU. The defining criterion for worker status is genuine and effective engagement in economic activity, and activity designed as a social reintegration measure does not meet that threshold.
key_quote: Work carried out under a social employment scheme … cannot be regarded as an effective and genuine economic activity.
full_summary: Bettray, a German national, participated in the Netherlands Social Employment Law scheme (WSW), which provided structured work placements for drug-dependent persons to assist their rehabilitation. He was paid but the work was specifically designed as a rehabilitation programme, not as ordinary employment. When the Netherlands sought to expel him, he claimed worker status under EU law. The CJEU held that he was not a 'worker' within the meaning of Article 45 TFEU: the activity was artificially created for rehabilitation purposes, not genuine economic activity in the labour market. This case is significant for FE-1 because it defines the outer limits of the Lawrie-Blum worker definition: while the CJEU broadly construes 'worker' (even part-time or low-paid activity can suffice), activity whose essential purpose is something other than economic participation—here, drug rehabilitation—does not qualify. Contrast with Steymann where quasi-commercial activity in a religious community did qualify.

case_name: Steymann v Staatssecretaris van Justitie
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A national of a Member State who performs genuine work within a religious community in return for accommodation, meals, and pocket money may qualify as a 'worker' under Article 45 TFEU, provided the work is genuine and effective economic activity and the remuneration takes the form of indirect benefits rather than wages. The indirect and unconventional nature of remuneration does not automatically preclude worker status.
key_quote: The activities performed … may be regarded as economic activities within the meaning of Article [45 TFEU] if they constitute … an indirect quid pro quo for the work performed.
full_summary: Steymann, a German national, joined the Bhagwan religious community in the Netherlands and performed plumbing and general maintenance work. In return he received accommodation, meals, and pocket money. The community operated several businesses. When he sought residence rights as a worker, the Netherlands denied them. The CJEU referred to the national court to determine whether his work was genuine and effective economic activity constituting a quid pro quo for his upkeep. If so, the indirect nature of the remuneration (no wage packet) would not prevent worker status. The case establishes that remuneration need not be conventional wages: benefits in kind, pocket money, or maintenance can satisfy the requirement of 'remuneration' for Article 45 purposes. Compare with Bettray: Steymann's work was genuinely economically productive, whereas Bettray's was rehabilitative. The distinction matters greatly for FE-1 questions on the scope of worker status.

case_name: Sotgiu v Deutsche Bundespost
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The prohibition on discrimination against workers from other Member States under Article 45 TFEU covers not only overt discrimination based on nationality but also covert (indirect) discrimination: rules that apply apparently neutral criteria which in practice have a disproportionate discriminatory effect on migrant workers are equally prohibited, unless objectively justified.
key_quote: The rules regarding equality of treatment … forbid not only overt discrimination by reason of nationality but also all covert forms of discrimination which, by the application of other criteria of differentiation, lead in fact to the same result.
full_summary: The German Federal Post Office paid a separation allowance to workers posted away from their home, but at a lower rate for workers whose permanent residence was abroad when first hired—a criterion that in practice applied almost exclusively to non-German (migrant) workers. Sotgiu, an Italian national hired while residing in Italy, challenged the lower allowance. The CJEU held that the principle of equal treatment in Article 45 TFEU prohibits indirect or covert discrimination: a criterion such as 'place of residence when hired' is facially neutral but in practice almost exclusively disadvantages migrant workers, so it must be objectively justified on non-national grounds to be compatible with EU law. The case is fundamental for FE-1 as the origin of the concept of indirect discrimination in the free movement context, later codified in Regulation 492/2011.

case_name: Angonese v Cassa di Risparmio di Bolzano SpA
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The prohibition on discrimination on grounds of nationality in Article 45 TFEU applies to rules laid down by private parties, not only to measures of public authorities. A private employer's requirement that job applicants hold a language certificate issued only by one institution in a specific province constitutes indirect discrimination against workers from other Member States and cannot be justified on objective grounds.
key_quote: The prohibition of discrimination … applies not only to the actions of public authorities but extends likewise to rules of any other nature aimed at regulating in a collective manner gainful employment.
full_summary: A bank in Bolzano (South Tyrol) required all job applicants to hold a specific bilingual (Italian/German) certificate only available from the local authority in Bolzano Province. Angonese, an Austrian-educated Italian national fluent in both languages, was refused permission to sit the selection exam because he lacked the specific certificate. He argued this breached Article 45 TFEU. The CJEU extended the direct horizontal effect of Article 45 to private employers, holding that the prohibition on nationality discrimination is not confined to state action. The certificate requirement indirectly discriminated against non-residents (mostly non-Italians) who could not easily obtain the Bolzano certificate, and could not be objectively justified. The case is significant for FE-1 in establishing the horizontal direct effect of Article 45 TFEU against private parties, an important extension of the Bosman line of cases.

case_name: R v Secretary of State for Employment, ex parte Seymour-Smith
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A rule of national law that requires a qualifying period of employment before a worker can bring an unfair dismissal claim constitutes indirect sex discrimination contrary to Article 157 TFEU (equal pay/treatment) if it adversely affects a considerably larger proportion of women than men, unless it can be shown to be objectively justified by reference to factors unrelated to sex. Statistical evidence must show a persistent and significant disparity.
key_quote: A considerably smaller proportion of women than men [are] able to fulfil the condition … that rule is … prima facie … indirectly discriminatory.
full_summary: The UK introduced a two-year qualifying period for unfair dismissal rights. The applicants argued that this disproportionately affected women, who were more likely to have shorter service periods due to childcare patterns. A reference was made to the CJEU. The CJEU held that national procedural rules on employment rights fall within the scope of Article 157 TFEU if they affect a significantly greater proportion of one sex. Statistical evidence needed to demonstrate a sustained and significant disparity; a temporary or marginal difference would not suffice. The national court was directed to consider whether the UK could objectively justify the rule (e.g., by labour market policy goals). The case is relevant for FE-1 in illustrating indirect sex discrimination doctrine under EU law, the role of statistical evidence, and the test for objective justification in employment law contexts.

case_name: Van Binsbergen v Bestuur van de Bedrijfsvereniging
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Article 56 TFEU (freedom to provide services) is directly effective and confers rights on individuals to provide and receive services across Member States. National rules requiring a service provider to be habitually resident in the Member State where the service is to be provided constitute a restriction on the freedom to provide services that can only be justified on overriding grounds of the public interest.
key_quote: Article [56 TFEU] … has direct effect … [and] restrictions … based on the place of establishment of the person providing the service cannot therefore be applied to a person who provides services and who is established in another Member State.
full_summary: Kortmann, a Dutch qualified legal representative, moved to Belgium mid-proceedings while representing Van Binsbergen in Dutch social security proceedings. Dutch law required legal representatives to be habitually resident in the Netherlands. Kortmann's fees were disputed on the basis he had lost his right to act. The CJEU held that Article 56 TFEU (then Art 59 EEC) was directly effective, conferring on individuals the right to provide services across borders. A residency requirement for service providers restricted that right. However, the Court accepted that a residency requirement might, in principle, be justified by the need to ensure that professional rules applicable to the service can be effectively enforced. On the facts, the Dutch requirement went beyond what was necessary. The case is foundational for FE-1 as the origin of direct effect for the services freedom and the first articulation of what became the 'rule of reason' for services restrictions.

case_name: Reyners v Belgian State
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The freedom of establishment in Article 49 TFEU is directly effective and can be relied upon by individuals before national courts even in the absence of implementing measures. National rules restricting access to a profession to nationals of that Member State violate Article 49 TFEU once the transitional period for the establishment of the common market has expired.
key_quote: The rule on equal treatment with nationals is one of the fundamental legal provisions of the Community … [it] became directly applicable in the legal systems of the Member States at the end of the transitional period.
full_summary: Reyners, a Dutch national trained as a Belgian lawyer, was refused admission to the Belgian Bar solely on nationality grounds, as Belgian law restricted the profession of avocat to Belgian nationals. He argued this breached EEC rules on freedom of establishment. The Belgian and other governments argued that the Treaty rules were not yet directly effective because implementing directives had not been adopted. The CJEU held that Article 49 TFEU (then Art 52 EEC) was directly effective upon expiry of the transitional period—the directive programme was merely a mechanism, not a condition, for the right. Member States could no longer use nationality as a criterion for access to self-employed activities after the transitional period. The case is of major constitutional significance for FE-1: it established that Treaty-based freedoms can be directly effective independent of secondary legislation, a principle that transformed the development of the internal market.

case_name: Klopp (Ordre des Avocats au Barreau de Paris v Klopp)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Article 49 TFEU precludes national bar rules that require a lawyer admitted to the bar of one Member State to close their existing chambers in that state before being allowed to establish a second set of chambers in another Member State. The freedom of establishment includes the right to establish a secondary establishment without abandoning the primary one.
key_quote: Community law … entitles a lawyer to practise his profession … not only in the Member State in which he has his principal place of business but also throughout the Community.
full_summary: Klopp, a German lawyer, sought admission to the Paris Bar while retaining his Düsseldorf chambers. The Paris Bar refused admission under its rules requiring a lawyer to practise from a single set of chambers, which effectively required abandonment of his German practice. The CJEU held that the 'single chambers' rule restricted freedom of secondary establishment under Article 49 TFEU. A lawyer established in one Member State has the right to establish a secondary office in another Member State without being required to give up the primary establishment. The Paris Bar's rule was not saved by the public interest exception because other means (e.g., professional responsibility rules, appointment of a local contact) could ensure proper representation of clients without a complete bar to secondary establishment. The case is important for FE-1 in clarifying that Article 49 protects both primary and secondary establishment, and that rules preventing multiple establishments across Member States are presumptively incompatible with EU law.

case_name: SPUC v Grogan
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The provision of abortion services for remuneration constitutes a 'service' within the meaning of Article 56 TFEU, as medical services provided for remuneration fall within the scope of the Treaty. However, where student unions distribute information about foreign abortion services without any economic link to the providers, they cannot rely on Article 56 TFEU to resist national injunctions, as they act outside the economic relationship on which Treaty rights are predicated.
key_quote: Medical termination of pregnancy … constitutes a service within the meaning of Article [56 TFEU].
full_summary: The Society for the Protection of Unborn Children (SPUC) sought an injunction in Ireland to restrain student unions from distributing information about abortion services available in the UK. The CJEU was asked whether this constituted a restriction on the freedom to receive and distribute information about services in another Member State. The Court confirmed that abortion performed for remuneration constitutes a 'service' under Article 56 TFEU. However, the student unions had no economic link with the UK clinics—they were distributing information as a matter of principle rather than commercial promotion. Without an economic nexus between the distributor and the service provider, Article 56 could not be invoked. The case is significant for Ireland in the context of the 8th Amendment debate and for EU law in drawing the boundary of the services freedom by reference to the economic relationship between the distributor of information and the service provider.

case_name: Geraets-Smits v Stichting Ziekenfonds / Peerbooms v Stichting CZ
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Medical services provided in a hospital setting in another Member State fall within the scope of Article 56 TFEU (freedom to provide services). National sickness insurance rules requiring prior authorisation before reimbursement of cross-border hospital treatment constitute a restriction on the freedom to receive services, but may be justified on grounds of maintaining a balanced and accessible healthcare system, subject to proportionality.
key_quote: Medical services provided for remuneration in a hospital are services within the meaning of Article [56 TFEU] notwithstanding that the activity is provided in the public sector.
full_summary: Geraets-Smits and Peerbooms sought reimbursement from their Dutch sickness funds for hospital treatment received in Germany and Austria respectively. Dutch rules required prior authorisation, granted only if treatment was considered 'normal' in professional circles and not adequately available in the Netherlands. The CJEU confirmed that hospital services fall within Article 56 TFEU even where performed in a public health system. The prior authorisation requirement restricted the freedom to receive services across borders. Justification was possible on grounds of planning and balancing the hospital system, as uncontrolled cross-border use could undermine the financial equilibrium of social security systems. However, the criteria for granting authorisation had to be based on objective, non-discriminatory, and pre-known criteria, applied proportionately. The case opened a significant chapter in EU health law and is relevant for FE-1 on the scope of the services freedom and permissible restrictions justified by overriding public interest requirements.

case_name: Dano v Jobcenter Leipzig
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The Citizens' Rights Directive 2004/38 does not require Member States to grant social assistance benefits to economically inactive EU citizens who move to another Member State without any intention of seeking employment. A Member State may lawfully exclude such persons from non-contributory benefits where they do not satisfy the conditions for legal residence under Directive 2004/38, particularly the requirement to have sufficient resources.
key_quote: Member States … may refuse to grant social assistance benefits to Union citizens who … do not have a right of residence on the basis of Directive 2004/38.
full_summary: Ms Dano, a Romanian national living in Germany with her son, had never been employed in Germany and made no attempt to seek work. She applied for German social security benefits (Hartz IV). The German court asked whether EU law required Germany to grant her these benefits. The CJEU held that Directive 2004/38 permits Member States to require economically inactive EU citizens to have sufficient resources as a condition of legal residence beyond three months. A citizen without sufficient resources does not have a right of residence under the Directive. Since she had no right of residence, she could not rely on the equal treatment principle in Article 24 of the Directive to claim social assistance on equal terms with nationals. The case marked a significant qualification of the expansive citizenship case law (Grzelczyk, Baumbast) by confirming that citizenship rights do not impose unconditional obligations on host states to fund all EU nationals regardless of economic ties.

case_name: Dereci v Bundesministerium für Inneres
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Article 20 TFEU (EU citizenship) may preclude a Member State from refusing a right of residence to a third-country national family member of a Union citizen where the refusal would oblige the Union citizen to leave EU territory, thereby depriving them of the genuine enjoyment of the substance of their citizenship rights. The test requires that the Union citizen would, in practice, be forced to leave the EU—mere inconvenience is insufficient.
key_quote: Article 20 TFEU precludes national measures … which have the effect of depriving Union citizens of the genuine enjoyment of the substance of the rights conferred by virtue of their status.
full_summary: Dereci, a Turkish national, entered Austria illegally and applied for a residence permit to remain with his Austrian wife and children—all Austrian nationals who had never exercised free movement rights. The Austrian authorities refused on the basis that Dereci had no EU law right to reside. The CJEU built on Ruiz Zambrano: Article 20 TFEU could grant a right of residence to a third-country national where the Union citizen would otherwise be forced to leave EU territory. However, the Zambrano exception is confined to situations where the Union citizen genuinely cannot remain in the EU without the third-country national—typically where the Union citizen is a minor dependent on a sole-carer third-country parent. An adult Union citizen who merely prefers family unity in the EU, and is not compelled to leave if the third-country family member is deported, does not trigger the Zambrano exception. The national court must assess whether the Union citizens are genuinely so dependent on Dereci that his expulsion would in fact cause them to leave. The case clarifies and limits the scope of Zambrano.

case_name: Adeneler v Ellinikos Organismos Galaktos (ELOG)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Where national law implementing a Directive is adopted before the Directive's implementation deadline, national courts must interpret pre-existing law consistently with the Directive's objectives only from the date of implementation, not from the Directive's entry into force. The obligation of consistent interpretation of national law with a Directive does not arise before the Directive's implementation deadline, to avoid placing disproportionate burdens on individuals through retroactive effect.
key_quote: The obligation on a national court to refer to the content of a directive when interpreting and applying the relevant rules of national law is limited … to the period after the period prescribed for implementation of that directive has expired.
full_summary: Adeneler was employed on a series of fixed-term contracts with ELOG, a Greek public body. He challenged the successive renewals as abusive under the Framework Agreement on Fixed-Term Work (annexed to Directive 1999/70/EC). A key issue was from when Greek courts were obliged to interpret national law consistently with the Directive, particularly as Greece had transposed it late. The CJEU clarified the temporal scope of the indirect effect obligation: national courts must interpret domestic law consistently with a Directive's objectives only from the expiry of the implementation deadline. Before that date, no such obligation arises (though courts may refrain from acting in a way that seriously compromises the Directive's purpose after the Directive enters force). The case also clarified what constitutes a 'fixed-term contract' and confirmed that repeated misuse of fixed-term contracts without objective justification can breach the Directive. Significant for FE-1 on the boundaries of the indirect effect doctrine in time.

case_name: Albany International BV v Stichting Bedrijfspensioenfonds Textielindustrie
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Collective agreements between employers and trade unions on working conditions, including supplementary pension schemes, fall outside the scope of Article 101 TFEU by virtue of the inherent nature of collective bargaining. Such agreements, concluded in the context of collective negotiations to improve conditions of employment, are excluded from the competition rules even if they restrict competition in the pensions market, given the social policy objectives of the Treaty.
key_quote: Collective agreements concluded between organisations representing employers and workers … do not, by reason of their nature and purpose, fall within the scope of Article [101(1) TFEU].
full_summary: Albany, a textile company, challenged the compulsory affiliation to a sectoral pension fund established by a collective agreement, arguing that it constituted an agreement restricting competition contrary to Article 101 TFEU and an abuse of a dominant position under Article 102. The CJEU developed the 'Albany exclusion': collective agreements between social partners which pursue social policy objectives fall outside Article 101 because the Treaty contemplates both competition and social policy, and these must be reconciled. Making every collective agreement subject to competition scrutiny would seriously undermine the right to collective bargaining. However, the exclusion is not unlimited—only agreements directly linked to the improvement of conditions of employment benefit from it. The case is important for FE-1 in identifying the tension between competition law and social policy, and in establishing a structural exclusion from Article 101 for genuine collective agreements.

case_name: Brasserie de Haecht SA v Wilkin (No 1)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: In assessing whether an exclusive supply ('beer tie') agreement has an appreciable effect on competition under Article 101(1) TFEU, the agreement must be considered in its economic and legal context, taking into account the cumulative effect of a network of similar agreements. A single agreement forming part of a bundle of similar ties may appreciably restrict competition even if that individual agreement would not do so in isolation.
key_quote: It is only by placing [the agreement] in its economic and legal context that it is possible to determine whether competition has been restricted, prevented or distorted.
full_summary: A Belgian café owner entered into an exclusive supply agreement with Brasserie de Haecht requiring the purchase of beer only from that brewery. When Wilkin sought to buy from another supplier, the brewery sued for breach of contract. The Belgian court asked the CJEU whether such an exclusive dealing tie constituted a prohibited agreement. The CJEU established the 'networks of agreements' or 'bundle effect' analysis: whether a single agreement restricts competition under Article 101(1) must be assessed within the total market context, including the cumulative effect of similar ties operated by many breweries across the sector. A tie that seems modest in isolation may appreciably restrict access to the market when viewed as part of a network of parallel agreements. The case established the contextual approach to appreciability that underpins modern foreclosure analysis in vertical restraints.

case_name: Beef Industry Development Society v Masterfoods Ireland Ltd
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: An agreement between competitors to reduce capacity (production rationalisation) falls within Article 101(1) TFEU as a restriction of competition 'by object' where it involves paying competitors to leave the market, even if the stated purpose is industry rationalisation. Such agreements cannot benefit from Article 101(3) exemption where they do not generate real productive efficiencies and their restrictions are unnecessary for any legitimate objective.
key_quote: An agreement … which restricts … competition between its parties by inducing some of them to leave the market cannot be justified by reference to Article [101(3) TFEU].
full_summary: The Beef Industry Development Society (BIDS) arranged for the main beef processors in Ireland to agree that certain members would exit the industry in return for levies from those remaining. The aim was to rationalise overcapacity in the Irish beef sector. The Irish courts referred to the CJEU whether such an agreement restricted competition by object. The CJEU held that an agreement inducing competitors to withdraw from the market in exchange for payment restricts competition by object under Article 101(1) TFEU: the very purpose is to reduce the number of suppliers, thereby altering market structure and reducing competitive rivalry. Justification under Article 101(3) was unavailable as there was no genuine efficiency gain—merely redistribution of market share. The case is important for FE-1 as it extended the 'by object' category to market exit agreements and as an example of Irish competition law intersecting with EU competition rules.

case_name: Société Technique Minière v Maschinenbau Ulm GmbH (STM)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Not every exclusive distribution agreement automatically falls within Article 101(1) TFEU; whether an agreement restricts competition must be determined by examining whether, without the agreement, competition would actually be feasible. An exclusive dealing agreement may escape Article 101(1) where it is necessary to enable a supplier to penetrate a new market and the exclusivity reflects the genuine risk undertaken by the distributor.
key_quote: In order to decide whether an agreement containing a clause 'granting an exclusive right of sale' must be regarded as prohibited … it is necessary to examine what competition would occur in the absence of the agreement.
full_summary: Maschinenbau Ulm granted STM exclusive distribution rights for its machines in France. STM sued for breach of contract; MU counterclaimed that the exclusivity was void under Article 101. The CJEU held that whether an exclusive distribution agreement violates Article 101(1) requires a contextual analysis of market conditions. The Court asked: would there be more competition without the agreement? If the supplier could not penetrate the French market without granting exclusivity to incentivise investment by the distributor, the clause might not restrict competition that otherwise would have existed—it may in fact enable competition that would not exist at all. This 'but for' competition analysis contrasts with the 'by object' approach and represents the 'by effect' methodology for assessing non-hardcore vertical restraints. STM established an important alternative route to compatibility with EU competition law that predates the current block exemptions.

case_name: Continental Can Co v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Article 102 TFEU may be used to control mergers by a dominant undertaking where the acquisition of a competitor eliminates effective competition in a substantial part of the internal market, even in the absence of specific EU merger control legislation. The abuse concept in Article 102 covers structural changes to the market that weaken competition.
key_quote: Abuse may … occur if an undertaking in a dominant position strengthens such a position in such a way that the degree of dominance … substantially fetters competition.
full_summary: Continental Can, a dominant US-based packaging manufacturer, acquired a major European competitor (Thomassen & Drijver-Verblifa) through its European subsidiary Europemballage. The Commission found this to be an abuse of dominant position under Article 102 TFEU. On appeal, the CJEU annulled the Commission's decision for insufficient market definition, but crucially confirmed the principle: a dominant undertaking that acquires a competitor and eliminates effective competition commits an abuse of Article 102, even though the Treaty did not then contain specific merger control rules. The case is historically important as it spurred the creation of the EU Merger Regulation (now ECMR) and established the principle that Article 102 has a structural dimension—it is not limited to predatory conduct but extends to structural changes that impair competition. Foundational for FE-1 in understanding the scope of Article 102 and EU merger control history.

case_name: ICI v Commission (Dyestuffs)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Parallel price increases by oligopolistic competitors constitute a concerted practice prohibited by Article 101 TFEU where they cannot be explained by independently reached decisions in response to market conditions and where coordination can be inferred from the sequence, uniformity, and timing of the increases. The 'concerted practice' concept captures informal coordination that falls short of a formal agreement.
key_quote: Although parallel behaviour may not by itself be identified with a concerted practice, it may however amount to strong evidence of such a practice if it leads to conditions of competition which do not correspond to the normal conditions of the market.
full_summary: Several dyestuffs manufacturers, including ICI, engaged in near-simultaneous and uniform price increases in the aniline dyes market. The Commission found a concerted practice. The CJEU upheld the finding, establishing the concept of 'concerted practice' as the third limb of the Article 101 prohibition (alongside agreements and decisions by associations). Where companies engage in conscious parallel behaviour that could only be explained by prior coordination rather than independent market responses, a concerted practice may be inferred. The case also applied the 'effects doctrine' in EU competition law, asserting jurisdiction over non-EU companies whose conduct produces effects within the EU. The case is significant for FE-1 in explaining what constitutes a 'concerted practice', the evidence needed to establish it, and the extraterritorial reach of EU competition law.

case_name: Suiker Unie UA v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The concept of concerted practice under Article 101 TFEU requires that each operator must determine independently the policy they intend to adopt on the market. Exchanging information with competitors about future conduct in a way that reduces uncertainty and enables each party to anticipate the market behaviour of the others constitutes a concerted practice, even in the absence of a formal agreement or meeting.
key_quote: The concept of a concerted practice … precludes any direct or indirect contact between such operators, the object or effect whereof is either to influence the conduct on the market of an actual or potential competitor or to disclose to such a competitor the course of conduct which they themselves have decided to adopt.
full_summary: EU sugar producers were found to have engaged in various forms of coordination including exchange of information about market operations, customer allocation, and territorial protection. The CJEU upheld most of the Commission's findings and significantly extended the definition of 'concerted practice': any direct or indirect contact that reduces strategic uncertainty about the behaviour of competitors constitutes a concerted practice, even without a formal meeting or written agreement. Mere participation in information exchange that enables operators to anticipate each other's actions violates Article 101. The judgment also established that the presumption that an undertaking which participated in anticompetitive information exchanges acted on that information in its market conduct (the 'Anic presumption') unless it proves otherwise. Foundational for understanding the scope of Article 101 concerted practices.

case_name: T-Mobile Netherlands BV v Raad van bestuur van de Nederlandse Mededingingsautoriteit
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A single exchange of information between competitors about future commercial conduct (including dealer remuneration) constitutes a concerted practice under Article 101 TFEU if it reduces strategic uncertainty and enables the parties to align their market conduct. It is not necessary to prove that the information exchange actually influenced subsequent market behaviour; a rebuttable presumption arises that undertakings which participated in the exchange took the information into account in determining their conduct.
key_quote: A concerted practice pursues an anticompetitive object for the purpose of Article [101(1) TFEU] where, having regard to its content and objectives and in the light of its legal and economic context, it is capable in an individual case of resulting in the prevention, restriction or distortion of competition.
full_summary: Mobile phone operators in the Netherlands met once to exchange information about reductions in standard dealer commissions for postpaid subscriptions. The NMa (Dutch competition authority) found a violation of Article 101. On reference, the CJEU confirmed that even a single meeting at which future commercial conduct is discussed can constitute a concerted practice with an anticompetitive 'object' if it reduces competitive uncertainty. It reaffirmed the Anic presumption: once it is shown that an undertaking participated in an information exchange, a rebuttable presumption arises that it took that information into account in its subsequent market behaviour. The operator bears the burden of rebutting this presumption. The case refined the object/effect distinction and the evidential presumptions in horizontal coordination cases, with significant implications for digital markets and algorithm-facilitated coordination.

case_name: Volk v Vervaecke
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: An agreement between undertakings falls outside Article 101(1) TFEU if the parties have an insignificant share of the relevant market and the agreement has no more than a negligible effect on trade between Member States and on competition. The de minimis rule prevents competition law from burdening minor commercial arrangements that cannot meaningfully restrict competition.
key_quote: An agreement falls outside the prohibition in Article [101] where it has only an insignificant effect on the markets, taking into account the weak position which the persons concerned have on the market.
full_summary: Volk, a small washing machine manufacturer with a market share of less than 1%, granted Vervaecke an exclusive distribution agreement for Belgium and Luxembourg. The CJEU held that an agreement with an appreciable effect on competition and inter-state trade is a prerequisite for the application of Article 101(1). Where the parties' combined market share is negligible, the agreement cannot appreciably restrict competition or affect trade between Member States. This de minimis principle has been developed further through Commission guidelines (the De Minimis Notice) specifying safe-harbour market share thresholds. Note: the de minimis exemption does not apply to 'by object' hardcore restrictions such as price-fixing or market allocation, even between small firms (see Commission De Minimis Notice 2014). The case is important for FE-1 in understanding the appreciability requirement and its limits.

case_name: Pronuptia de Paris GmbH v Pronuptia de Paris Irmgard Schillgallis
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Franchise agreements do not as such fall within Article 101(1) TFEU; clauses that are essential to protect know-how and the identity and reputation of the franchise network are compatible with competition law.
