However, clauses that fix prices or allocate markets between franchisees are hardcore restrictions prohibited by Article 101(1) and cannot benefit from exemption under Article 101(3).
key_quote: Provisions which are strictly necessary in order to ensure that the know-how and assistance provided by the franchisor do not benefit competitors do not constitute restrictions on competition for the purposes of Article [101(1) TFEU].
full_summary: Pronuptia, a wedding dress franchisor, granted an exclusive franchise for three German cities. Schillgallis refused to pay fees, arguing the franchise agreement violated Article 101. The CJEU established a framework for analysing franchise agreements: they have two categories of clause. (1) Clauses protecting the uniformity and reputation of the network (e.g., use of brand, quality control, location approval) are necessary for the franchise system to exist and do not restrict competition. (2) Clauses that divide markets between franchisees or fix retail prices are hardcore restrictions that cannot be saved. Since franchise agreements generate genuine efficiencies and allow independent entrepreneurs to operate under a proven brand without starting from scratch, they deserve favourable treatment. The case is foundational for EU vertical restraints analysis and for FE-1 in understanding the structured approach to franchise arrangements.

case_name: Expedia Inc v Autorité de la concurrence
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: An agreement that restricts competition 'by object' under Article 101(1) TFEU is by definition capable of appreciably affecting competition and trade between Member States, even where the parties' market shares fall below the thresholds in the Commission's De Minimis Notice. The de minimis principle does not apply to hardcore 'by object' restrictions.
key_quote: An agreement that may affect trade between Member States and that has an anti-competitive object constitutes, by its nature and independently of any concrete effect that it may have, an appreciable restriction on competition.
full_summary: SNCF and Expedia entered into an online travel portal agreement. The French competition authority found a violation. On reference, the CJEU clarified that the Commission's De Minimis Notice does not protect agreements with anticompetitive object from Article 101 TFEU: by-object restrictions inherently and appreciably restrict competition, regardless of the parties' market shares. Only restrictions 'by effect' need to meet the appreciability threshold. This is a significant rule: companies cannot rely on small market shares to avoid Article 101 liability for price-fixing, market-sharing, or other hardcore restrictions. The case reinforces the bifurcated approach to Article 101 analysis and is important for FE-1 in understanding when market share thresholds are and are not relevant.

case_name: GlaxoSmithKline Services v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: An agreement need not restrict competition between the parties to an agreement to infringe Article 101(1) TFEU; it is sufficient that it restricts competition between the parties and third parties, or restricts the freedom of one party in a way that has anticompetitive effects on the market. The consumer welfare element is relevant to Article 101(3) analysis but does not affect the classification of an agreement as a restriction by object under Article 101(1).
key_quote: Article [101(1) TFEU] protects … not only the interests of competitors or of consumers but also the structure of the market and, in so doing, competition as such.
full_summary: GSK adopted a dual-pricing system for pharmaceutical products in Spain: lower prices for domestic sale, higher prices for export, aimed at reducing parallel imports from Spain. The Commission found this violated Article 101. GSK argued that the system benefited consumers by maintaining incentives for pharmaceutical R&D. The CJEU confirmed that the agreement restricted competition by object (limiting parallel imports) regardless of GSK's efficiency arguments, which were relevant only at the Article 101(3) stage. The Court also clarified that Article 101 protects the competitive structure of the market—not only immediate consumer welfare—as a proxy for long-term consumer benefit. An agreement restricting parallel trade, even where the supplier argues innovation benefits, is presumptively anticompetitive in object. Important for FE-1 on the relationship between Article 101(1) and (3).

case_name: Philip Morris International v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The acquisition by one undertaking of a minority shareholding in a competitor can fall within Article 101(1) TFEU where the acquisition serves as an instrument for influencing the commercial conduct of the target and restricts competition between them. A structural link that enables one competitor to influence the other's competitive strategy may constitute an anticompetitive agreement even if it is not a full merger.
key_quote: Where … the acquisition of a shareholding in a competitor … affects the possibilities of competition and thus the operation of the market, it falls within Article [101 TFEU].
full_summary: Philip Morris sought to acquire a stake in Rothmans, its competitor in the tobacco market. The Commission examined whether this minority acquisition was compatible with EU competition rules. The CJEU held that Article 101 TFEU can apply to agreements for the acquisition of minority shareholdings in competitors where the acquisition creates structural links enabling the acquirer to influence the competitive behaviour of the target. The case predated the EU Merger Regulation and illustrated the gap in the pre-ECMR regime. It is still relevant in the context of partial acquisitions not meeting merger thresholds and for understanding the outer reach of Article 101 to encompass structural instruments of competitive coordination.

case_name: AKZO Chemie BV v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A dominant undertaking that engages in predatory pricing—selling below average variable cost, or below average total cost where prices are set as part of a plan to eliminate a competitor—commits an abuse of dominant position under Article 102 TFEU. A market share of 50% or above gives rise to a rebuttable presumption of dominance.
key_quote: Prices below average variable costs … must be regarded as abusive. Prices below average total costs but above average variable costs must be regarded as abusive if they are determined as part of a plan for eliminating a competitor.
full_summary: AKZO, a major chemicals company dominant in the organic peroxides market, was found to have engaged in predatory pricing against ECS, a smaller rival, by selectively cutting prices on customers serviced by ECS to below cost while maintaining higher prices elsewhere. The CJEU established the two-limb AKZO test for predatory pricing under Article 102: (1) prices below average variable cost are presumptively predatory, as no rational firm would sell at such prices unless seeking to drive out a rival; (2) prices between AVC and ATC are predatory if part of a deliberate strategy to eliminate competition. The case also confirmed the 50% market share presumption of dominance (now widely applied). Foundational for FE-1 competition law and EU predatory pricing analysis.

case_name: NV Nederlandsche Banden-Industrie Michelin v Commission (Michelin I)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A dominant undertaking has a 'special responsibility' not to allow its conduct to impair undistorted competition on the internal market. Loyalty rebate schemes that tie dealers to the dominant supplier by means of year-end bonuses contingent on achieving sales targets constitute an abuse of dominant position under Article 102 TFEU, even if they do not directly coerce the dealer, because they create a fidelity-inducing effect that forecloses competition.
key_quote: A dominant undertaking … has a special responsibility not to allow its conduct to impair genuine undistorted competition on the common market.
full_summary: Michelin NV held a dominant position in new replacement tyres for heavy vehicles in the Netherlands. It operated a complex annual rebate system under which dealers received year-end bonuses conditional on meeting individual sales targets set by Michelin. Dealers thus had an incentive to favour Michelin throughout the year so as not to lose the entire bonus. The Commission found an abuse. The CJEU held that a dominant undertaking bears a 'special responsibility': its conduct is subject to stricter scrutiny. The target-based rebate scheme, while not outright coercive, created a strong commercial incentive for dealers to remain loyal to Michelin, thereby foreclosing rivals. Michelin I established the fidelity rebate doctrine: rebates conditional on achieving sales targets by a dominant firm are presumptively abusive under Article 102. Later developed in Michelin II and Intel.

case_name: Oscar Bronner GmbH v Mediaprint Zeitungs-und Zeitschriftenverlag GmbH
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Refusal by a dominant undertaking to grant access to its infrastructure (essential facility) constitutes an abuse under Article 102 TFEU only where: (1) the refusal eliminates all competition in the downstream market; (2) the refusal cannot be objectively justified; and (3) the facility is indispensable in the sense that there is no actual or potential alternative, even one less advantageous. The essential facilities doctrine has a high threshold and should not be invoked to require dominant firms to subsidise competitors.
key_quote: It is sufficient … to examine whether there are services which can constitute alternative solutions, even if they are less advantageous … to conclude whether the … facility is truly indispensable.
full_summary: Mediaprint operated the only nationwide home-delivery newspaper distribution system in Austria and refused to allow Oscar Bronner's newspaper to use it. Bronner argued this was a refusal to deal amounting to an abuse of dominant position. The CJEU established the essential facilities doctrine but set a demanding threshold: the facility must be truly indispensable (no alternative, even technically inferior), the refusal must eliminate all effective competition downstream, and objective justification must be absent. The Court found that while Mediaprint was dominant, Bronner could in principle develop its own delivery system (or use postal services), so indispensability was not established. The case limits the essential facilities doctrine, avoiding imposing mandatory sharing of infrastructure that would reduce incentives to invest. Important for FE-1 in balancing competition law with property rights and investment incentives.

case_name: Intel Corporation v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Exclusivity rebates by a dominant firm are not automatically unlawful under Article 102 TFEU without further analysis. Where the dominant undertaking produces evidence showing that the rebates were incapable of restricting competition (applying the as-efficient competitor test), the Commission must assess all the circumstances of the rebate scheme, including its ability to foreclose an as-efficient rival, before finding an abuse.
key_quote: If … the undertaking submits, during the administrative procedure … that its conduct was not capable of restricting competition … the Commission is obliged to examine those arguments.
full_summary: Intel, the dominant CPU manufacturer, provided loyalty rebates to PC manufacturers and major retailers conditional on purchasing all or substantially all CPUs from Intel, thereby foreclosing AMD, its main rival. The Commission imposed a then-record fine. The CJEU overturned the General Court's ruling affirming the Commission's per se approach to exclusivity rebates. The CJEU held that exclusivity rebates should not be treated as automatically abusive—they are 'by their nature likely' to restrict competition but that presumption can be rebutted. Where the dominant firm submits evidence based on the as-efficient competitor (AEC) test, the Commission must engage with it and conduct a thorough effects analysis. The judgment marked a move from a formalistic to an effects-based approach to Article 102 rebate cases and is highly significant for FE-1 in understanding the evolving analytical framework under Article 102.

case_name: France Télécom SA v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Predatory pricing by a dominant undertaking constitutes an abuse of dominant position under Article 102 TFEU even where the dominant firm had no reasonable prospect of recouping the losses sustained during the predation period. A finding of predatory pricing does not require proof of recoupment; the harm to competition and consumers arises from the elimination of competition itself.
key_quote: A finding of predatory pricing does not require, in all cases, proof that the undertaking concerned had a realistic chance of recouping its losses.
full_summary: Wanadoo (a subsidiary of France Télécom), dominant in the French ADSL internet access market, priced its services below full cost and, during one period, below variable cost. The Commission found predatory pricing abuse. On appeal, France Télécom argued that predatory pricing should only be unlawful if the dominant firm has a realistic chance of recouping its losses (following US antitrust precedent in Brooke Group). The CJEU rejected the recoupment requirement for EU Article 102 purposes: the primary aim of Article 102 is to protect the competitive structure of the market; the harm is the foreclosure of competition itself, not merely the long-run risk of price increases after a recoupment phase. The no-recoupment-requirement rule is a significant divergence from US antitrust law and is important for FE-1 in understanding differences between EU and US competition law approaches.

case_name: Post Danmark A/S v Konkurrencerådet (Post Danmark I)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A dominant undertaking that charges prices below average total cost but above average incremental cost to selective customers does not automatically abuse its dominant position under Article 102 TFEU, unless it is shown that the pricing strategy has or is likely to have an anticompetitive exclusionary effect on the relevant market, taking into account all circumstances. The as-efficient competitor test and effects-based analysis should be applied.
key_quote: Not every exclusionary effect is necessarily detrimental to competition … only conduct which forecloses competition from undertakings that are at least as efficient as the dominant undertaking is abusive.
full_summary: Post Danmark, the dominant postal operator in Denmark, offered selective price reductions to three former customers of its rival Forbruger-Kontakt, at prices below average total cost but above average incremental cost. The CJEU held that selective pricing by a dominant firm, even at below-ATC prices, is not automatically abusive. The correct test is whether the pricing has an actual or likely exclusionary effect on as-efficient competitors. Factors include price-cost analysis, the duration of pricing, market share, barriers to entry, and whether the as-efficient competitor would be foreclosed. Post Danmark I extended the effects-based approach from Intel's rebate analysis to pricing below ATC. Together with AKZO, the case defines the modern EU framework for predatory pricing analysis. Relevant for FE-1 as it demonstrates the move toward effects-based analysis in Article 102 cases.

case_name: Barber v Guardian Royal Exchange Assurance Group
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Occupational pension benefits and contracted-out pension benefits constitute 'pay' within the meaning of Article 157 TFEU (then Art 119 EEC). Setting different pensionable ages for men and women for the purposes of occupational pensions violates the principle of equal pay. Due to the major financial implications, the temporal effect of the ruling was limited to future cases and claims already pending.
key_quote: Benefits paid by an employer to a worker in connection with the latter's compulsory redundancy fall within the scope of Article [157 TFEU] … it is contrary to Article [157 TFEU] to impose an age condition which differs according to sex.
full_summary: Mr Barber was made redundant at age 52 under a contracted-out pension scheme. Female employees in equivalent situations could draw an immediate pension at 50; males had to wait until 55. He argued that paying him less by deferring his pension violated Article 157 TFEU. The CJEU held that occupational pension benefits fall within the concept of 'pay' under Article 157 TFEU and that differential pensionable ages based on sex violate the equal pay guarantee. This ruling had enormous financial implications for pension schemes throughout the EU. To mitigate disruption, the CJEU limited the temporal effect of the ruling: Article 157 TFEU could only be relied upon for benefits attributable to periods of service after 17 May 1990 (the date of the judgment), except for claimants who had already initiated proceedings. The temporal limitation of direct effect is of special importance for FE-1 in understanding how the CJEU manages the practical consequences of rulings that upset entrenched expectations.

case_name: Bilka-Kaufhaus GmbH v Weber von Hartz
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: An employer's practice of excluding part-time workers from an occupational pension scheme constitutes indirect sex discrimination contrary to Article 157 TFEU where significantly more women than men work part-time, unless the employer can demonstrate that the exclusion is objectively justified by reasons unrelated to sex and corresponds to a genuine need of the enterprise that is appropriate and necessary to achieve a legitimate business aim.
key_quote: A measure … which excludes part-time employees from an occupational pension scheme does not contravene Article [157 TFEU] … if the undertaking is able to show that … measures are explained by objectively justified factors unrelated to any discrimination based on sex.
full_summary: Bilka, a German department store, ran an occupational pension scheme that excluded part-time workers. Mrs Weber von Hartz, a long-serving part-time employee, argued this violated Article 157 TFEU. The CJEU held that the exclusion constituted indirect sex discrimination (significantly more women than men work part time) and was therefore caught by Article 157 TFEU unless Bilka could objectively justify it. The Court established the Bilka justification test: the measure must (a) correspond to a real need of the enterprise, (b) be appropriate to the objective pursued, and (c) be necessary to achieve that objective. This three-part objective justification test for indirect sex discrimination in employment became the standard framework for EU equal pay law. The case is central for FE-1 questions on indirect discrimination and employer defences in employment equality law.

case_name: Enderby v Frenchay Health Authority
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Where statistics demonstrate a significant difference in pay between two job groups of different sex, the burden of proof shifts to the employer to provide objective justification unrelated to sex, even where the pay difference results from separate collective bargaining arrangements. The fact that pay levels were determined by collective bargaining does not of itself constitute objective justification for pay differences between predominantly female and predominantly male groups.
key_quote: Where significant statistics disclose an appreciable difference in pay between two jobs of equal value … the burden of proof is on the employer to show that the difference is based on objectively justified factors unrelated to any discrimination based on sex.
full_summary: Ms Enderby, a speech therapist employed by the NHS, received substantially less pay than male comparators in the same employer's service working as pharmacists and clinical psychologists. Both groups had pay set by separate collective bargaining rounds; neither was directly discriminatory. She argued that the significant statistical gap constituted indirect pay discrimination. The CJEU held that where statistics reveal a significant pay difference between groups of predominantly different sexes, a prima facie case of discrimination arises and the burden shifts to the employer to justify the difference. The fact that each group's pay was fixed through separate collective bargaining does not constitute objective justification—collective bargaining can itself perpetuate historical discrimination. The Enderby burden-shifting rule is central to EU and Irish equal pay litigation and critical for FE-1.

case_name: Dekker v Stichting Vormingscentrum voor Jong Volwassenen
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Refusal to employ a woman because she is pregnant constitutes direct sex discrimination under EU equal treatment law, since only women can be pregnant. An employer cannot rely on the financial consequences of maternity (e.g., inability to claim reimbursement from an insurance fund) as justification for refusing to employ a pregnant woman. No comparator of the opposite sex is required where the discriminatory ground is unique to one sex.
key_quote: A refusal of employment on account of pregnancy … can only affect women and therefore constitutes direct discrimination on grounds of sex.
full_summary: Ms Dekker applied for a post as a training instructor. Although she was the most suitable candidate, the employer refused to appoint her because she was pregnant and the employer could not recover maternity pay from its insurance fund, leaving it financially exposed during her absence. The CJEU held that refusing to employ a pregnant woman is direct sex discrimination—no comparator is needed. The financial consequences of hiring a pregnant woman (inability to obtain insurance reimbursement) could not justify discrimination because to allow such justification would empty the equal treatment principle of its effect. The case established that pregnancy discrimination is per se sex discrimination under EU law, a principle later codified in the Pregnant Workers Directive 92/85/EC. Fundamental for FE-1 in EU employment equality law.

case_name: Hauer v Land Rheinland-Pfalz
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The right to property is a fundamental right protected as a general principle of EU law; restrictions on that right may be justified by objectives of general interest pursued by the Community, provided that the restrictions do not impair the essence of the right and correspond to the objective pursued in a proportionate manner. EU fundamental rights are informed by constitutional traditions common to the Member States and international treaties to which Member States are parties.
key_quote: The right to property is guaranteed in the Community legal order … subject to certain limitations justified by the objectives of general interest pursued by the Community.
full_summary: Mrs Hauer owned land in Germany suitable for viticulture. An EC Regulation imposed a temporary ban on new vine plantings as part of wine market structural policy. She argued this violated her fundamental right to property and her freedom to pursue a trade or profession. The CJEU held that fundamental rights form an integral part of EU general principles of law and that EU measures must respect them. The right to property and the right to pursue an economic activity are included. However, restrictions on these rights are permissible if they (a) serve general EU public interest objectives, (b) do not impair the essential core of the rights, and (c) are proportionate. On the facts, the planting ban was a time-limited structural measure serving the EU's wine market policy objective and was proportionate. Hauer is foundational for FE-1 in establishing that EU law has its own fundamental rights catalogue drawn from the constitutional traditions of Member States—a doctrine later codified in the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights.

case_name: Kücükdeveci v Swedex GmbH & Co KG
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The general principle of non-discrimination on grounds of age, now expressed in the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights (Article 21) and implemented by Directive 2000/78/EC, has horizontal direct effect in disputes between private parties and must be applied by national courts even where it requires the disapplying of contrary national legislation. National courts need not first make a preliminary reference before disapplying national law incompatible with a directly effective EU principle.
key_quote: The principle of non-discrimination on grounds of age … must be interpreted as precluding national legislation … [and] it is for the national court to ensure the full effectiveness of the general principle of non-discrimination on grounds of age by disapplying any provision of national law contrary to that principle.
full_summary: Ms Kücükdeveci worked for Swedex from the age of 18. When dismissed after 10 years, German law required that periods of employment before age 25 be disregarded when calculating the notice period, so her notice was calculated as if she had only worked 3 years. The CJEU held that this German provision violated the EU general principle of non-discrimination on grounds of age (reflected in Art 21 CFR and Directive 2000/78/EC). Crucially, the Court held that this general principle—unlike a Directive standing alone—can produce horizontal direct effect in disputes between private parties. National courts must disapply domestic legislation that conflicts with this principle without waiting for a preliminary reference. The case extends the Mangold line of cases and has significant implications for horizontal application of EU fundamental rights in employment contexts. Very important for FE-1 on the horizontal effect of EU fundamental rights principles.

case_name: Åklagaren v Åkerberg Fransson
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU applies whenever Member States act within the scope of EU law (Article 51(1) CFR). Criminal proceedings for tax fraud fall within the scope of EU law where the fraud relates to VAT obligations, which are governed by EU Directives. The ne bis in idem principle in Article 50 CFR prohibits a Member State from bringing criminal proceedings for VAT fraud against a person who has already been penalised administratively for the same acts.
key_quote: The fundamental rights guaranteed in the legal order of the European Union are applicable in all situations governed by European Union law, but not outside such situations.
full_summary: Mr Åkerberg Fransson, a Swedish fisherman, faced tax surcharges for failing to declare VAT income. He was then prosecuted criminally for the same acts. He argued the criminal prosecution violated the ne bis in idem (double jeopardy) principle in Article 50 CFR. The CJEU first confirmed that the Charter applies whenever Member States implement EU law—including where national tax measures implement EU VAT obligations. On the substance, a combination of administrative tax surcharges and criminal prosecution for the same offence can violate Article 50 CFR if both procedures are criminal in nature (applying the Engel criteria from the ECHR). National courts must assess whether the tax surcharge was of a criminal character. Åkerberg Fransson is pivotal for FE-1 in clarifying the scope of the Charter's application (Art 51 CFR) and the ne bis in idem principle under EU law.

case_name: Mangold v Helm
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The general principle of non-discrimination on grounds of age is a general principle of EU law, predating and independent of Directive 2000/78/EC. National courts must disapply national provisions that contradict this principle, even before the expiry of the Directive's implementation period, to ensure the effectiveness of EU fundamental rights law.
key_quote: The principle of non-discrimination on grounds of age must … be regarded as a general principle of Community law.
full_summary: German law allowed a lower threshold for concluding fixed-term contracts with workers over 52, ostensibly to promote employment. Mr Mangold, aged 56, was employed on a fixed-term contract under these provisions. He challenged this as age discrimination. The CJEU held that, although Directive 2000/78's implementation period had not yet expired, the non-discrimination on grounds of age is a general principle of EU law deriving from constitutional traditions and international instruments. National courts must give effect to this principle and disapply conflicting national law, even outside the Directive's framework. Mangold was controversial as it effectively accelerated the application of an unimplemented Directive and claimed an exceptionally broad scope for age discrimination protection. It was later confirmed in Kücükdeveci and is foundational for understanding the CJEU's constitutionalisation of equality law. Very significant for FE-1 on general principles and their relationship to Directives.

case_name: Bauer and Willmeroth v Broßonn
citation: Joined Cases C-569/16 and C-570/16 [2018] ECLI:EU:C:2018:871
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Article 31(2) of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights, which guarantees every worker the right to paid annual leave, has horizontal direct effect and can be relied upon directly by individuals against private employers. National legislation or contract terms that deprive workers of accrued paid annual leave on death breach Article 31(2) CFR and must be disapplied by national courts in disputes between private parties.
key_quote: Article 31(2) of the Charter has direct effect … in a dispute between individuals [and] a national court … must disapply any national provision … which precludes … the right to paid annual leave.
full_summary: In these joined cases, the widows of deceased workers sought payment in lieu of paid annual leave their late husbands had accrued but not taken before death. German law, as interpreted by domestic courts, extinguished the right to paid annual leave on death, denying payment to the estates. The CJEU confirmed that the right to paid annual leave in Article 31(2) CFR is sufficiently precise and unconditional to have direct effect—crucially, including horizontal direct effect against private employers. This extended the principle from Kücükdeveci (age discrimination, Art 21 CFR) to social rights in Article 31(2) CFR. National courts must disapply domestic legislation that breaches Article 31(2) even in private party disputes. The case advanced the direct horizontal applicability of Charter social rights and is important for FE-1 on the new frontier of Charter direct effect.

case_name: Egenberger v Evangelisches Werk für Diakonie und Entwicklung eV
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Articles 21 (non-discrimination) and 47 (right to an effective remedy) of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights have direct horizontal effect and must be applied by national courts in disputes between individuals, requiring disapplication of national law that does not allow effective judicial scrutiny of a religious organisation's claim to exemption from anti-discrimination rules for occupational activities.
key_quote: Articles 21 and 47 of the Charter … can be relied upon in a dispute between individuals … and the court … must … ensure the judicial protection of the rights that individuals derive from EU law.
full_summary: Ms Egenberger applied for a fixed-term position with a Protestant diaconal organisation for work drafting a report on the UN Convention on Racial Discrimination. The organisation rejected her application partly because she had no religious affiliation. She claimed discrimination on grounds of religion contrary to Directive 2000/78. The CJEU held that Article 4(2) of the Directive (allowing religious organisations to require membership of the religion) must be subject to effective judicial scrutiny—it is not for the religious employer alone to determine whether religion is a genuine and justified occupational requirement. To give effect to this requirement, Articles 21 and 47 CFR (non-discrimination and right to a remedy) have direct horizontal effect that national courts must apply. The case expanded horizontal CFR rights beyond age (Mangold, Kücükdeveci) into religion and the right to an effective remedy.

case_name: Siragusa v Regione Sicilia
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The Charter of Fundamental Rights applies only when Member States act 'within the scope of Union law' under Article 51(1) CFR. Purely national measures that do not implement EU law, or are not otherwise governed by EU law, fall outside the Charter's scope. The connection with EU law must be more than tenuous; there must be a real and specific link between the national measure and EU law.
key_quote: The concept of 'implementing Union law' … requires a certain degree of connection above and beyond the matters covered being closely related or one of those matters having an indirect impact on the other.
full_summary: Mr Siragusa built a terrace on his villa in Sicily without planning permission, in a protected landscape area. Italian administrative law required demolition. He argued this breached his right to property under the CFR. The CJEU, however, held that the Charter did not apply: Italian landscape protection legislation was not an implementation of EU law. Although there are EU environmental directives, national landscape planning rules are not the implementation of those Directives. The connection was too tenuous. The case is important for FE-1 in defining the limits of the Charter's scope: the Charter does not apply every time a national measure touches on an area where EU law also exists—there must be a genuine and specific connection between the national measure and the implementation of EU law. This limits the risk of the Charter converting all national law into EU law.

case_name: Minister for Justice and Equality v Poplawski (No 2)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Where national law is incompatible with a directly effective provision of EU law, national courts must disapply the conflicting national provision without waiting for it to be removed by the legislature or declared unconstitutional. The primacy of EU law requires disapplication in concrete cases before national courts, regardless of the form of the national norm (statute, constitution, etc.).
key_quote: It follows from the primacy of EU law … that any … national court is obliged to disapply any provision of national law which is contrary to EU law.
full_summary: Poland sought the extradition of Mr Poplawski from the Netherlands under a European Arrest Warrant for drug offences. The Dutch court refused on the basis that he was a Dutch resident and Dutch law provided for alternative enforcement. The question was whether Dutch law complied with the Framework Decision on the EAW. When the CJEU found an incompatibility, the Dutch court asked whether it could apply the Framework Decision directly. The CJEU reiterated the full implications of the supremacy doctrine: even where a legal instrument (here a Framework Decision under the old third pillar) lacks direct effect, national courts must still disapply conflicting national law and interpret domestic law consistently with the EU instrument as far as possible. If consistent interpretation is impossible, national law must be disapplied. The case is significant for FE-1 in confirming that supremacy requires disapplication, not merely consistent interpretation, and extends to EU instruments beyond regulations and directives.

case_name: Da Costa en Schaake NV v Nederlandse Belastingadministratie
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A national court of last instance is relieved of its obligation to refer a question to the CJEU under Article 267(3) TFEU where the question raised is materially identical to one already decided by the CJEU in a prior case. The prior ruling deprives the question of its character as a 'question' requiring resolution; the answer is already provided by acte éclairé.
key_quote: The authority of an interpretation [already given by the Court] … may deprive the obligation of its purpose and thus empty it of its substance.
full_summary: Three Dutch import companies raised the same question about tariff interpretation on similar products as had been decided in Van Gend en Loos. The CJEU held that, because it had already answered the materially identical question, the national court was not bound to refer again. The obligation to refer (Art 267(3) TFEU) is designed to prevent divergent national interpretations of EU law; where the CJEU has already given the authoritative answer, the obligation loses its purpose. Da Costa thus established the 'acte éclairé' doctrine: a previous CJEU ruling on the identical or equivalent question releases the national court from the referral obligation. This doctrine is closely linked to CILFIT (acte clair) and both are central for FE-1 in understanding the dynamics of the preliminary reference procedure.

case_name: Foglia v Novello (No 1)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The CJEU has jurisdiction under Article 267 TFEU only to answer questions that are genuinely necessary for the resolution of a real dispute between parties in adversarial litigation. The Court may refuse jurisdiction where the reference is contrived or the parties share the same interest in obtaining a particular ruling on EU law, as the Court's jurisdiction is not designed to deliver advisory opinions on hypothetical problems.
key_quote: The duty assigned to the Court by Article [267 TFEU] is not that of delivering advisory opinions on general or hypothetical questions but of assisting in the administration of justice in the Member States.
full_summary: Foglia and Novello were both Italian and contrived a dispute in which Novello agreed to a contract term that would allow them to challenge a French import tax on liqueurs. The Italian court referred the question. The CJEU declined jurisdiction, holding that the parties had arranged a fictional dispute specifically to obtain a ruling on French tax law in circumstances where no genuine controversy existed between them. The Court has no jurisdiction to give advisory opinions or to rule on questions manufactured to challenge the laws of another Member State through a sham dispute. The case was controversial—critics argued the Court was improperly refusing a valid reference. Foglia No 2 (Case 244/80) confirmed the refusal. The case is important for FE-1 in defining the limits of the Court's jurisdiction under Article 267 TFEU and the requirement for a genuine dispute.

case_name: Lyckeskog
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A court is a 'court or tribunal of a Member State against whose decisions there is no judicial remedy under national law' within Article 267(3) TFEU only if its decisions cannot be appealed by way of right. Where a supreme court requires leave to appeal and such leave may be refused, the lower court whose decisions are subject to that discretionary leave procedure is not the court of last instance and has a discretion—not an obligation—to refer.
key_quote: The fact that examination of appeals on points of law against decisions of [a court] is subject to a prior declaration of admissibility … does not deprive parties of judicial protection.
full_summary: Mr Lyckeskog was convicted by a Swedish court for illegally importing rice. The case eventually came before the Hovrätten (Court of Appeal). The Swedish Supreme Court (Högsta Domstolen) required leave to appeal. The CJEU held that the Hovrätten was not a 'court of last instance' for Article 267(3) purposes because a further avenue of appeal (with leave) existed. The existence of a filtering mechanism for supreme court appeals does not make the court of appeal the court of last resort—parties retain judicial access to the highest court, and that court bears the Article 267(3) obligation to refer. The case is important for FE-1 in defining which court bears the obligation to refer, particularly in systems that use leave-to-appeal filtering mechanisms common in modern judicial systems.

case_name: Atlanta Fruchthandelsgesellschaft mbH v Bundesamt für Ernährung und Forstwirtschaft
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: National courts may grant interim relief suspending the application of a national measure that implements an EC regulation where: (1) the court has serious doubts about the validity of the EU measure; (2) the matter is urgent; (3) the applicant would suffer serious and irreparable damage; and (4) the national court takes into account the Community interest. The court must also make a preliminary reference to the CJEU on validity unless one has already been made.
key_quote: A national court which has serious doubts about the validity of a Community measure … may grant interim relief and must refer the question of validity to the Court of Justice.
full_summary: Atlanta challenged EU banana import regulations, arguing they were unlawful. It sought interim suspension of the national implementing measures. The CJEU, building on Zuckerfabrik (Cases C-143/88 and C-92/89), confirmed the conditions under which national courts can grant interim measures suspending the application of national measures that implement EU law they doubt to be valid. The Court stressed that national courts cannot simply declare EU measures invalid—only the CJEU can do that under Foto-Frost. However, national courts can grant temporary interim relief while a validity reference is made and pending. This safeguards effective judicial protection while preserving the CJEU's monopoly on invalidity rulings. Relevant for FE-1 in understanding the complementary roles of national courts and the CJEU and the coherent system of judicial protection in the EU.

case_name: TWD Textilwerke Deggendorf GmbH v Bundesrepublik Deutschland
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: An individual who was entitled to challenge a Commission Decision directly before the General Court (then CFI) under Article 263 TFEU (then Art 173 EEC) but failed to do so within the time limit may not subsequently raise the invalidity of that decision by way of a preliminary reference under Article 267 TFEU. Allowing a collateral attack on a decision that the claimant could and should have challenged directly would undermine the legal certainty protected by the strict time limits in Article 263 TFEU.
key_quote: The national court is bound to take account of … the fact that [the applicant] … could have challenged [the decision] before the Court of Justice without any doubt … but chose not to do so.
full_summary: The Commission ordered Germany to recover State aid paid to TWD Textilwerke. Germany informed TWD of this decision and the company failed to bring an action for annulment within the Article 263 TFEU time limit. Later, when Germany sought to recover the aid, TWD sought to challenge the Commission's decision indirectly through national court proceedings and a preliminary reference on validity. The CJEU held that where an individual had undoubted standing to challenge a Commission decision directly under Article 263 and allowed the time limit to expire, it cannot later rely on the invalidity of that decision in Article 267 proceedings. The TWD doctrine protects legal certainty: the strict 2-month annulment time limit under Article 263 would be rendered meaningless if parties could circumvent it through indirect challenges. Important for FE-1 in understanding the relationship between direct actions and preliminary references.

case_name: Meroni & Co, Industrie Metallurgiche SpA v High Authority
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The Treaty establishes a specific institutional balance among the Community institutions, and no institution may delegate to a body outside the Treaty framework powers wider than those it possesses itself. Delegation of wide discretionary powers—as opposed to clearly defined executive powers subject to judicial review—upsets the institutional balance and violates the Treaty.
key_quote: A delegation of powers to a body established outside the Treaty must … be limited to clearly defined executive powers, the use of which must … be entirely subject to the supervision of the authority delegating those powers.
full_summary: The ECSC High Authority delegated powers concerning scrap metal equalisation to two private Belgian agencies. Meroni challenged decisions of those agencies. The CJEU held that the High Authority could not delegate powers wider than its own and could not delegate discretionary powers to a private body not subject to the same judicial and democratic controls as Treaty institutions. Only narrowly defined executive powers strictly subject to review could be validly delegated. The Meroni doctrine underpins EU institutional design: it limits agencies' and private bodies' powers to executive functions with no true discretion. It is invoked in challenges to the powers of EU agencies (EBA, ESMA, etc.) and is foundational for FE-1 in understanding EU institutional law and the limits of agency powers.

case_name: Commission v Council (ERTA)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The Community has implied external treaty-making competence in areas where it has legislated internally (the ERTA doctrine). Once the Community has exercised its internal competence to legislate in a field, Member States lose the power to enter into international agreements with third countries in that field, as doing so could affect the Community rules or alter their scope.
key_quote: Each time the Community … adopts provisions laying down common rules, whatever form these may take, the Member States no longer have the right … to undertake obligations with third countries which affect those rules.
full_summary: The Commission challenged a Council decision authorising Member States to negotiate and conclude the European Road Transport Agreement (ERTA) with third countries, arguing that external transport competence now belonged to the Community following the adoption of Community road transport legislation. The CJEU established the doctrine of implied external competence (the ERTA or faeris principle): where the Community has exercised legislative competence internally, it acquires exclusive external competence in that area, precluding unilateral Member State action. This principle is now codified in Article 3(2) TFEU. The case is fundamental for EU constitutional law and the FE-1 on the division of internal and external competences and the process by which exclusive competence is acquired.

case_name: Codorniu SA v Council
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A Regulation, despite being a generally applicable measure, may be of direct and individual concern to a private applicant for the purposes of Article 263 TFEU (then Art 173 EEC) where the applicant can demonstrate that it is singled out from all other persons by reason of attributes peculiar to it or by reason of circumstances that differentiate it from all others. Legislative measures are not immune from challenge by individuals who are individually concerned.
key_quote: Although [the Regulation] is legislative in nature … this does not prevent it from being of individual concern to [Codorniu] … by reason of attributes peculiar to them.
full_summary: Codorniu, a Spanish sparkling wine producer, held a registered trademark 'Gran Cremant' which it had used since 1924. An EC Regulation reserved the term 'crémant' exclusively for certain French and Luxembourg wines, preventing Codorniu from using its traditional designation. Codorniu sought annulment under Article 263 TFEU. The CJEU found that although the Regulation was general legislation, Codorniu was individually concerned because the regulation specifically affected its individual trademark right that differentiated it from all other traders. The case extended the Plaumann formula of individual concern beyond purely administrative decisions to legislative measures. This is significant for FE-1 as it opened a limited avenue for private parties to challenge EU legislative acts under Article 263 TFEU, before the more extensive reforms introduced by the Lisbon Treaty's regulatory act provisions.

case_name: IBM Corporation v Commission
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Only measures that produce definitive legal effects capable of affecting the interests of the applicant are reviewable 'acts' under Article 263 TFEU. Preparatory measures, procedural steps, and measures not yet producing binding legal effects are not reviewable—they may only be challenged when the final decision is made. The admissibility of annulment actions requires a binding act that definitively alters the legal position of the applicant.
key_quote: Only measures … which produce binding legal effects of such a kind as to affect the interests of the applicant by bringing about a distinct change in his legal position are acts … within the meaning of Article [263 TFEU].
full_summary: IBM was subject to a Commission investigation for alleged abuse of dominant position. The Commission sent IBM a statement of objections and a letter saying it intended to commence formal proceedings. IBM sought to challenge these steps as acts reviewable under Article 263 TFEU, to prevent the proceedings from beginning. The CJEU held that only measures producing definitive legal effects are reviewable acts. Preliminary procedural steps (letters of intent, statements of objections, decisions to open proceedings) do not definitively change the legal position of the addressee—they are preparatory acts. IBM could challenge the final Commission decision but not interim steps. This case defines the threshold for reviewable 'acts' under Article 263 TFEU and is important for FE-1 in understanding the conditions for judicial review of EU acts, particularly in competition law enforcement.

case_name: Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami v European Parliament and Council
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Under Article 263(4) TFEU (as amended by the Lisbon Treaty), private parties may challenge 'regulatory acts' not entailing implementing measures without meeting the Plaumann individual concern test. However, 'regulatory acts' within Article 263(4) TFEU are non-legislative acts of general application; legislative acts (adopted through the ordinary or special legislative procedure) still require the Plaumann test for individual concern.
key_quote: The concept of 'regulatory act' … must be understood as covering all acts of general application apart from legislative acts.
full_summary: Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami and other Inuit organisations challenged an EU Regulation banning the marketing of seal products, arguing it breached their fundamental rights and was disproportionate. The Lisbon Treaty amended Article 263(4) TFEU to add a third limb allowing challenges to regulatory acts without implementing measures without proof of individual concern. The CJEU held that this new limb applies only to non-legislative acts of general application (regulatory acts), not to Regulations adopted through the ordinary legislative procedure (OLP), which remain 'legislative acts.' Since the seal products ban was adopted via the OLP, it was a legislative act and the applicants still needed to demonstrate individual concern under the strict Plaumann test—which they could not do. The case limits the Lisbon Treaty's expansion of individual access to judicial review and is significant for FE-1 in understanding the post-Lisbon standing rules under Article 263 TFEU.

case_name: Germany v Council (Tobacco Advertising Directive)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A measure adopted under the internal market legal basis (Article 114 TFEU, then Art 95 EC) must genuinely contribute to eliminating obstacles to free movement or distortions of competition in the internal market. Legislation that is primarily aimed at protecting public health without substantively affecting internal market conditions cannot validly be based on Article 114 TFEU, as it would improperly extend EU competence into areas reserved to Member States.
key_quote: A measure adopted on the basis of Article [114 TFEU] must genuinely have as its object the improvement of the conditions for the establishment and functioning of the internal market.
full_summary: The EU adopted a Directive prohibiting virtually all advertising of tobacco products, relying on the internal market legal basis. Germany challenged the Directive arguing that public health was its true aim, not internal market integration. The CJEU annulled the Directive, holding that while some provisions (e.g., advertising in cross-border publications) genuinely addressed free movement issues, others (blanket bans on sports sponsorship, outdoor advertising) were not connected to internal market conditions. A Directive that merely incidentally relates to the internal market while primarily pursuing a different aim (public health) cannot rely on Article 114 TFEU. The case is highly significant for FE-1 in understanding EU competence limits, the correct legal basis for EU legislation, and the principle that the EU may not simply invoke the internal market to regulate any area it wishes.

case_name: Campus Oil Ltd v Minister for Industry and Energy
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: A national requirement that oil importers purchase a percentage of their requirements from a State-owned oil refinery at regulated prices constitutes an MEQR under Article 34 TFEU, but may be justified under Article 36 on public security grounds where the Member State can demonstrate that maintaining the refinery is essential to its security of petroleum supply and that less restrictive alternatives are unavailable.
key_quote: Products intended to ensure the survival of the national oil refinery … may fall within the exception of Article [36 TFEU] on grounds of public security.
full_summary: Irish legislation required petroleum importers to purchase at least 35% of their requirements from the Irish National Petroleum Corporation's Whitegate refinery at government-set prices. Campus Oil challenged this as an MEQR under Article 34 TFEU. The CJEU confirmed the measure was an MEQR. However, Ireland argued that, as a small island state without domestic oil production, maintaining refining capacity was essential to its energy security. The CJEU held that 'public security' in Article 36 TFEU encompasses petroleum supply security because oil is fundamental to the existence and functioning of a modern state and its essential services. The measure was held to be potentially justified, subject to proportionality: it had to be the minimum necessary to ensure the refinery's viability and Ireland's supply security. Campus Oil is the only case in which public security under Article 36 TFEU has been successfully invoked to justify an MEQR, and it is frequently cited in Irish EU law courses.

case_name: Crotty v An Taoiseach
court: Supreme Court of Ireland
legal_principle: The Irish Government cannot ratify an international treaty that effects a fundamental alteration of the constitutional framework of the State, or transfers sovereignty in a way not already authorised by the Constitution, without first amending the Constitution by referendum. The Single European Act required a constitutional amendment (the Third Amendment) before Ireland could ratify it, as it extended Community competence in ways that altered the structure of government envisaged by the Constitution.
key_quote: The first part of Title III of the Single European Act is such as to alter fundamentally the nature of the Community … and the relationship between Ireland and the Community, as to require an amendment of the Constitution.
full_summary: Crotty, a horse breeder, challenged the Government's attempt to ratify the Single European Act (SEA) without a constitutional referendum. The Supreme Court held that the constitutional basis for Ireland's EU membership (Article 29.4 of Bunreacht na hÉireann) authorised ratification of treaties that were consistent with the original understanding of Community membership. However, the SEA introduced new political cooperation mechanisms and extended Community powers beyond what was authorised by the existing constitutional provision. The Government therefore had to obtain a referendum to amend the Constitution before ratifying the SEA. The case is foundational in Irish constitutional law for the principle that Treaty amendments that expand EU competence require constitutional authorisation by referendum. It is the reason every major EU Treaty amendment (Maastricht, Amsterdam, Nice, Lisbon) required a referendum in Ireland and has been applied repeatedly. Highly relevant for FE-1 on the relationship between EU law and the Irish Constitution.

case_name: Maher v Minister for Agriculture
court: Supreme Court of Ireland
legal_principle: EU Regulations are part of Irish domestic law by virtue of Article 29.4 of the Constitution (the EU membership clause) and do not require separate national implementing legislation. The constitutional immunity from challenge granted by Article 29.4 covers acts done by the State that are necessitated by its EU membership obligations, including the implementation of EU Regulations and the exercise of powers conferred by EU law.
key_quote: Under Article 29.4 … the State may act … in a manner inconsistent with [the Constitution] if necessitated by the obligations of membership.
full_summary: Maher challenged the milk quota regime established by EU Regulations implemented in Ireland by ministerial orders, arguing that it violated property rights protected by the Constitution. The Supreme Court held that the milk quota system flowed directly from EU Regulations and that action taken by the State to give effect to EU Regulations is constitutionally protected by Article 29.4 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. The constitutional guarantee of property rights could not be used to undermine directly applicable EU Regulations. The case confirmed that Ireland's constitutional EU membership clause provides broad immunity to EU-derived measures from constitutional challenge, subject to the fundamental rights exception potentially arising if the EU itself fails to protect fundamental rights (the Solange-type caveat discussed but not applied). Significant for FE-1 in understanding how the Irish Constitution accommodates the primacy of EU law.

case_name: Meagher v Minister for Agriculture
court: Supreme Court of Ireland
legal_principle: The European Communities Act 1972 constitutes a valid delegation of legislative power to ministers and other bodies to implement EU law by statutory instrument (secondary legislation), notwithstanding the general constitutional principle that legislative power may not be sub-delegated without Oireachtas authority. The implementation of EU obligations is an exception to the general non-delegation principle given the constitutional status of EU membership.
key_quote: The European Communities Act … constitutes a valid exercise of the legislative power of the Oireachtas … the authority granted … to make regulations … is within constitutional limits.
full_summary: Meagher challenged a ministerial regulation implementing an EU Directive, arguing that the European Communities Act 1972—which allowed ministers to implement EU obligations by statutory instrument—was itself an unconstitutional delegation of Oireachtas legislative power to the executive. The Supreme Court rejected this challenge, holding that the European Communities Act 1972 validly delegated legislative power to ministers for the purposes of implementing EU obligations, within the constitutional framework created by Article 29.4 of Bunreacht na hÉireann. The case confirmed the constitutionality of the primary implementation mechanism used in Ireland to bring EU Directives into domestic law. It is important for FE-1 in understanding the domestic constitutional architecture for implementing EU law in Ireland.

case_name: Grad v Finanzamt Traunstein
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Decisions addressed to Member States may have direct effect if they are clear, precise, unconditional, and leave no room for discretion, allowing individuals to rely on them before national courts. The binding legal force of a Decision is not limited to its addressee State; it can also be relied upon by individuals where the conditions for direct effect are met.
key_quote: It would be incompatible with the binding effect attributed to a Decision … to exclude in principle the possibility that persons affected may invoke the obligation imposed by that Decision.
full_summary: Grad was a carrier who argued that a Council Decision required Germany to apply certain VAT rules to his transport contracts. Germany argued that a Decision addressed to a Member State could not have direct effect. The CJEU held that, where a Decision imposes a clear, unconditional obligation on a Member State, individuals who benefit from that obligation may rely on it before national courts. The case established that direct effect is not confined to Treaty provisions and Regulations but extends to Decisions as well, subject to the usual criteria. It is significant for FE-1 in illustrating the breadth of the direct effect doctrine across different EU legal instruments, and as one of the first post-Van Gend cases to extend direct effect beyond Treaty provisions.

case_name: Variola SpA v Amministrazione delle Finanze dello Stato
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: EU Regulations are directly applicable in all Member States without the need for any implementing measures; indeed, national measures purporting to 'transpose' a Regulation into domestic law are prohibited if they obscure the EU nature of the provision or alter its scope. Member States may not enact national legislation that reproduces or replaces EU Regulations, even by way of administrative simplification.
key_quote: Member States are under the obligation not to introduce any measure which might affect the jurisdiction of the Court … or which might obscure the directly applicable character of Community Regulations.
full_summary: Italy enacted a national decree that reproduced customs duty provisions originally contained in an EU Regulation. Variola argued that the Italian decree, rather than the Regulation, was the source of the obligation. The CJEU held that EU Regulations are directly applicable under Article 288 TFEU: they do not require national implementing measures and their direct applicability cannot be undermined by domestic reproduction. If a Member State re-enacts a Regulation in national legislation, individuals may be misled about the EU origin of the rule and the CJEU's jurisdiction to interpret it. Reproduction is therefore prohibited. The case is foundational for understanding the direct applicability of Regulations and the distinction between Regulations (which may not be reproduced) and Directives (which must be implemented). Important for FE-1 on the hierarchy and forms of EU legal acts under Article 288 TFEU.

case_name: Larsy v INASTI
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Where a national authority's application of national law in a manner incompatible with EU law results in damage to an individual, State liability under the Francovich/Brasserie du Pêcheur doctrine may arise even where the breach involves an administrative body applying its own interpretation of conflicting national and EU rules, provided the breach is sufficiently serious.
key_quote: State liability is also engaged where … an administrative body consistently applied national provisions in a manner which … was contrary to Community law.
full_summary: Mr Larsy, a Belgian self-employed worker, sought certain pension benefits. INASTI, the Belgian social security institute, refused on the basis of national provisions that the CJEU subsequently held were incompatible with EU social security coordination rules. Larsy argued INASTI's wrongful application of incompatible Belgian law triggered State liability. The CJEU confirmed that the Francovich State liability principle applies not only to failures of national legislatures but also to wrongful conduct by national administrative bodies that breach EU law in ways causing damage. The key question was whether the breach was 'sufficiently serious,' which is assessed by examining whether the administrative body had a genuine choice or excusably misread complex EU law. The case extends the State liability doctrine into administrative decision-making and is relevant for FE-1 in understanding the reach of the Francovich remedy.

case_name: Inter-Environnement Wallonie ASBL v Région Wallonne
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: During the period between a Directive's entry into force and the expiry of its implementation deadline, Member States must refrain from taking measures liable seriously to compromise the attainment of the Directive's result. This is an obligation of good faith under Article 4(3) TEU and arises even before any obligation to achieve the Directive's result has fully materialised.
key_quote: Member States … must refrain from taking any measures liable seriously to compromise the result prescribed by [the Directive] … during the period before the deadline.
full_summary: The Belgian region of Wallonia adopted waste regulations during the period when a Waste Framework Directive had been adopted but whose implementation period had not yet expired. Inter-Environnement Wallonie argued that those measures conflicted with the future Directive. The CJEU held that, while Member States are not yet obliged to have implemented the Directive before the deadline, they must nonetheless refrain from enacting legislation that would seriously jeopardise attainment of the Directive's objectives. This standstill obligation derives from the duty of sincere cooperation under Article 4(3) TEU. The case is important for FE-1 on the temporal scope of Directive obligations and the intermediate duty owed by Member States between a Directive's publication and its implementation deadline (later refined in Adeneler).

case_name: Unilever Italia SpA v Central Food SpA
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: Technical standards regulations that have not been notified to the Commission as required by Directive 83/189/EEC (now 2015/1535/EU) are unenforceable against individuals in proceedings between private parties. The failure to notify is a procedural defect that renders the national provision inapplicable in that dispute, creating an exclusionary horizontal effect for the notification obligation.
key_quote: The [notification] obligation is capable of being relied on by individuals before national courts … [and] breach … renders the technical regulations inapplicable.
full_summary: Unilever supplied olive oil to Central Food in packaging that complied with EC rules but not with an Italian labelling decree that had not been notified to the Commission under the technical standards notification procedure. Central Food refused to pay, claiming the goods did not meet Italian requirements. The CJEU held that Italy's failure to notify the labelling decree rendered it inapplicable: the national measure could not be relied upon against Unilever. Crucially, unlike Directives (which lack horizontal direct effect under Marshall), the exclusion of unnotified national technical regulations operates even in private party disputes—not by granting rights to Unilever (positive horizontal effect) but by preventing reliance on the unlawful national rule (exclusionary horizontal effect). The case refines the taxonomy of direct effect and is important for FE-1 on indirect horizontal effect mechanisms.

case_name: Viking Line ABP v International Transport Workers' Federation (ITF)
court: Court of Justice of the European Union
legal_principle: The right to take collective action (including strike action) is a fundamental right recognised in EU law, but it may constitute a restriction on freedom of establishment or freedom to provide services under the Treaty. Such collective action must be justified by an overriding reason of public interest (social policy objectives) and must be proportionate; collective action designed to prevent re-flagging of a vessel to a lower-cost Member State must be assessed for proportionality against the legitimate aim of protecting workers.
key_quote: The right to take collective action for the protection of workers is a legitimate interest which, in principle, justifies a restriction on one of the fundamental freedoms guaranteed by the Treaty.
full_summary: Viking Line, a Finnish ferry operator, sought to re-flag its vessel Rosella under Estonian registration to reduce labour costs. The ITF and Finnish Seamen's Union threatened or took collective action to prevent this, as it would allow Viking to apply the lower Estonian collective agreement. The CJEU held that the freedom of establishment (Article 49 TFEU) applied to Viking's reorganisation and could be relied on against collective action by trade unions (horizontal direct effect of establishment freedom). The right to strike is a fundamental right that can in principle justify restrictions on free movement. However, collective action must be necessary and proportionate to the social objective pursued—collective action designed simply to maintain all terms and conditions irrespective of the destination Member State's standards may go too far. The case raised intense controversy about the relationship between social rights and internal market freedoms. Highly significant for FE-1 and for understanding EU social law tensions.
